Is the Healthcare Industry Digitally Fit? Our Survey Findings

Key Statistics:
- 4 million downloads each app
- 33% of healthcare providers are digitally mature
- Consumers are taking charge of their health using digital tools

A Comparison of Healthcare Providers by Digital Maturity

Digitally Mature Healthcare Providers:
- 58% use social media to offer services to customers
- 48% use mobile channels to personalize services
- 47% use digital technologies to personalize care
- 78% have a common vision across senior management for digital transformation
- 84% allocate adequate funding to digital transformation
- 63% have integrated business processes
- 94% have an integrated view of data
- 84% are building digital skills

Non-Digitally Mature Healthcare Providers:
- 18% use social media to offer services to customers
- 18% use mobile channels to personalize services
- 21% use digital technologies to personalize care
- 28% have a common vision across senior management
- 31% allocate adequate funding to digital transformation
- 31% have integrated business processes
- 33% have an integrated view of data
- 38% are building digital skills

How Can Healthcare Providers Move Up the Digital Curve?

1. Define a Vision and Secure Top Management Buy-in
- Define transformation goals
- Assess existing digital initiatives
- Set up a dedicated Digital Services Unit (DSU)

2. Establish a Transformation Roadmap and Governance Model to Drive Digital Initiatives
- Identify quick wins and long-term digital initiatives
- Build a digital communication backbone
- Leverage insights from digital initiatives

3. Promote Internal Collaboration and Knowledge Sharing to Drive Internal Engagement
- Training personnel
- Hire experienced digital practitioners
- Streamline operations

Sources: National Research Corporation, Consumers Going Digital With Their Healthcare Experiences Report March 2014
Based on 1,200 survey responses across 13 US healthcare providers
Source: Capgemini Consulting Analysis

[Footer includes social media icons and Capgemini Consulting branding]

- Lack of integration between mental health and criminal justice systems

- Improved mental health treatment access
- Better integration of health systems

- Increased funding for mental health services

EPIDEMIC

Although gun violence in America is frequently attributed to the availability of firearms, another relevant factor is our nation's broken mental health care system. Although most people who suffer from mental health disorders are nonviolent, severe mental health disorders that go untreated can increase the risk for violence. Thus, if we hope to curb gun violence, we must de-stigmatize and treat mental health disorders.

GUN VIOLENCE IN AMERICA

2/3 OF ALL MASS KILLINGS INVOLVE GUNS.

There were 154 mass killings from 2006 to 2010.

In America, a mass killing — defined as having 4 victims, not including the killer — occurs once every 2 weeks.

THOSE INCLUDE
774 VICTIMS

OF THOSE VICTIMS,
161 WERE CHILDREN

GUNS & SUICIDE

31,000
GUN RELATED DEATHS IN THE UNITED STATES

5% OF ALL SUICIDE ATTEMPTS ARE BY GUNSHOT.

19,000 OF THOSE ARE SELF INFLICTED

85% OF THOSE ATTEMPTS RESULTED IN DEATH.

Suicide is the 11th leading cause of death in the United States and the third-leading cause of death for people ages 10–24 years.

In July 2007, a nationwide report indicated that male veterans are twice as likely to die by suicide as compared to their civilian peers.

ACCESS TO GUNS

FEDERAL GUN LAWS CURRENTLY ENACTED INCLUDE:

- Prohibition of firearms sales or transfers to those suffering from mental health disorders and others

- Federal background checks on firearms purchases from licensed sellers; records of which must legally be destroyed after 24 hours

- Prohibition of firearms possession in school zones, unless stored in a locked container

- Prohibition of fully automatic weapon sales to civilians

- Prohibition of possession of a firearm by persons convicted of felonies

- Prohibition of carrying a handgun without a permit in some states

BUT GUN CONTROL IS LARGELY DICTATED BY INDIVIDUAL STATES, CREATING LARGE DISCREPANCIES BETWEEN STATES. FOR EXAMPLE, SOUTH DAKOTA HAS SOME OF THE WEAKEST GUN LAWS AND CALIFORNIA HAS SOME OF THE STRONGEST.

SOUTH
DAKOTA
- No law authorizing the reporting of mental health adjudications to the National Instant Criminal Background Check System (NICS)
- No license to purchase firearms
- No waiting period on firearm purchases
- No restrictions on concealed carry permit
- No magazine capacity restriction
- No firearm registration

CALIFORNIA
- Gun dealers must be licensed
- Waiting period and background checks required for all handgun purchases
- Assault weapons are restricted
- Magazine capacity restrictions
- Gun shows require licensed dealers
- Permit required to carry concealed weapons
- No open carry

Gun control laws are further weakened by discrepancies in regulations for licensed firearms dealers, which are stricter, and regulations for private sales, which are more lax.

MENTAL HEALTH AND GUN VIOLENCE

7 MILLION
Americans suffer from severe mental health disorders, such as schizophrenia, acute depression or bipolar disorder.

The increased risk of violence for most mentally ill people is minimal, but when paired with issues like substance abuse and left untreated, the risk is more than doubled.

HALF OF U.S. ADULTS WILL DEVELOP A MENTAL ILLNESS DURING THEIR LIFETIME.

People with severe mental illness are roughly twice as likely to commit violent acts in their lifetime.

THE FLOUNDERING MENTAL HEALTH SYSTEM

LESS THAN HALF OF AMERICANS WITH SEVERE MENTAL HEALTH ISSUES RECEIVE ADEQUATE TREATMENT.

$1.6 BILLION in state mental health services have been cut since 2009.

These cuts led to significant reductions in hospital and community services for those with serious mental illness, including cutbacks to:
HOUSING
OUTPATIENT INTERVENTIONS
MEDICATION
CRISIS SERVICES

13%
of children ages 8 to 15 live with severe mental health disorders

BUT ONLY

20%
of those children receive treatment

ONE-HALF OF ALL LIFETIME CASES OF MENTAL ILLNESS BEGIN BY AGE 14. YET THERE ARE LONG DELAYS — SOMETIMES DECADES — BETWEEN THE ONSET OF SYMPTOMS AND WHEN PEOPLE SEEK AND RECEIVE TREATMENT.

500,000:
TOTAL BEDS AVAILABLE IN AMERICAN MENTAL INSTITUTIONS AT PEAK LEVELS IN THE 1950s

43,000:
TOTAL BEDS CURRENTLY AVAILABLE

This shortage of inpatient psychiatric care makes finding appropriate care difficult, if not impossible.

IN 2011
24 STATES
SLASHED MENTAL HEALTH PROVIDER REIMBURSEMENT RATES FOR MEDICAID — THE MOST IMPORTANT SOURCE OF FUNDING OF PUBLIC MENTAL HEALTH SERVICES.

REAL
SOLUTIONS

"We must not only tackle gun control but we must also fix deficiencies in the U.S. mental health system, gaps that can leave many people with serious mental illnesses and family members without an easy way to get the treatment and support we know can help avert catastrophes."
Lynn R. Goldman, pediatrician and dean of the George Washington University School of Public Health and Health Services

New Assault Weapons Ban
Senator Dianne Feinstein has authored the Assault Weapons Ban of 2013, which would ban 157 military-style weapons and large-capacity magazines.

Funding Mental Health Care
The recently passed Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act will expand Medicaid coverage and include requirements to cover mental health care. It will also increase federal support for community-based services and require parity for mental health benefits.

Gun Violence Research
On January 16, President Barack Obama signed an executive order directing the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention to research causes of gun violence and preventative measures.

Destigmatizing Mental Illness
On February 4, Department of Health & Human Services Secretary Kathleen Sebelius announced an initiative to end discrimination against people with mental illnesses, expand access to care, and to integrate mental health with primary care to help people seek help and keep people from seeking care.

[By country]
Bangladesh: GDP Growth, Poverty, Sanitation Marketing, Household Subsidies
Indonesia: GDP Growth, Poverty Decline, Sanitation Marketing, Policy Supported Public-Private Partnerships
Peru: GDP Growth, Poverty Decline, Private Sector Interest in Sanitation Promotion, Dissatisfaction with Current Solutions

- Unmet Customer Needs
- Weak Supply Chain
- Difficult Investment Climate

- Build business capacity and professionalism
- Encourage formation of strategic partnerships to increase supply and distribution channels to reach remote customers

GDP GROWTH     POVERTY DECLINE     SANITATION MARKETING     UNSATISFIED SANITATION NEEDS

MARKET DRIVERS

WHAT ARE CONSTRAINTS TO INVESTMENT?

UNMET CUSTOMER NEEDS
"Why should I pay for a toilet or latrine?"
"Why should I settle if I can't afford the dream?"

The poor are challenging customers who would rather make do with their current sanitation situation if they cannot find a desirable, affordable product in their local market.

WEAK SUPPLY CHAIN
"How do I reach poor households?"
"Why should I prioritize sanitation in my portfolio?"

The sanitation market is highly fragmented and uncoordinated, making it difficult to develop and deliver products that consumers are willing and able to buy. Enterprises with the scale to tackle these industry challenges do not currently see sanitation or the poor as priority markets; businesses that do focus on sanitation have limited reach and product differentiation.

DIFFICULT INVESTMENT CLIMATE
"How can we improve the ease of doing business in the sanitation sector?"
"Why should we invest in this even riskier business?"

Current sanitation policies are not focused on lifting barriers to market entry. Despite the existence of government sanitation promotion programs, sanitation enterprises do not have enough information about viable markets and lack sufficient access to finance.

KEY CHALLENGES

RECOMMENDATIONS

1 Stimulate Demand
- Show consumers that good sanitation is a smart economic decision
- Design sanitation solutions that meet poor customers' needs and preferences
- Offer flexible financing options

2 Strengthen the Sanitation Supply Chain
- Build business capacity and market intelligence
- Encourage formation of strategic partnerships to develop stronger distribution channels to reach remote customers

3 Improve the Investment Climate
- Communicate clear policies that promote improved sanitation
- Facilitate private sector provision by clearly defining the role of the private sector in government strategy and policy

The poor are challenging customers who would rather make do with their current sanitation situation if they cannot find a desirable, affordable product in their local market.

WEAK SUPPLY CHAIN
"How do I reach poor households?"
"Why should I prioritize sanitation in my portfolio?"
The sanitation market is highly fragmented and uncoordinated, making it difficult to develop and deliver products that consumers are willing and able to buy. Enterprises with the scale to tackle these industry challenges do not currently see sanitation or the poor as priority markets; businesses that do focus on sanitation have limited reach and product differentiation.

DIFFICULT INVESTMENT CLIMATE
"How can we improve the ease of doing business in the sanitation sector?"
"Why should we invest in this even riskier business?"
Current sanitation policies are not focused on lifting barriers to market entry. Despite the existence of government sanitation promotion programs, sanitation enterprises do not have enough information about viable markets and lack sufficient access to finance.

KEY CHALLENGES

RECOMMENDATIONS

1 Stimulate Demand
- Show consumers that good sanitation is a smart economic decision
- Design sanitation solutions that meet poor customers' needs and preferences
- Offer flexible financing options

2 Strengthen the Sanitation Supply Chain
- Build business capacity and market intelligence
- Encourage formation of strategic partnerships to develop stronger distribution channels to reach remote customers

3 Improve the Investment Climate
- Communicate clear policies that promote improved sanitation
- Facilitate private sector provision by clearly defining the role of the private sector in government strategy and policy

For more information, visit www.wsp.org/dpsp

Reality Stone

BUSINESS ANALYTICS vs DATA SCIENCE

In the age of big data, parsing unwieldy amounts of information can lead to world-changing innovation.

To understand this data, companies are hiring a variety of specialists, including business analysts and data scientists.

Who Are They?

BUSINESS ANALYSTS
Research and extract valuable information from structured and unstructured sources to explain historical, current, and future business performance; determine the best analytical models and approaches to present and explain solutions to business users.

DATA SCIENTISTS
Design, develop, and deploy algorithms through statistical programming that support business decision-making tools; manage large amounts of data; create visualizations to aid in understanding.

What Education Do They Have?

The majority of business analysts come from a variety of backgrounds including business and humanities, while data scientists tend to come from computer science, mathematics, and technology backgrounds.

BUSINESS ANALYSTS
30% Bachelor's
56% Master's
11% Doctoral

DATA SCIENTISTS
28% Bachelor's
48% Master's
20% Doctoral

Business Analysts
- Business Administration
- Information Technology
- Finance
- Political Science
- Anthropology
- Economics
- History
- Psychology

Data Scientists
- Mathematics
- Computer Science
- Information Science
- Computer Software Engineering
- Technical Communications
- Computer Information Systems
- Statistics

What Skills Do They Have?

Both business analysts and data scientists are experts in using data to inform decisions, but they apply their skills in different ways, using the same or similar tools. The skills listed below describe someone who has earned a master's degree in the field.

Business Analysts
Data Scientists

Decision-Making
- Instigate change by transforming business insights from interdisciplinary data analysis into tangible resources for business decision-making.
- Discover the opportunity in the datasets by mining data and writing machine learning algorithms to support decision-making process.

Applied Problem Solving
- Define business problems and translate statistical analysis into data-driven business intelligence that improves business performance.
- Create the foundation of analysis by which an applied business problem can be solved.

Data Analysis
- Research, interpret, and visualize raw data using predictive, prescriptive and descriptive analysis to make it useful and accessible for business users.
- Manage and organize large sets of multivariate data using methods like linear discriminant analysis and multilinear regression selection.

Analytical Models
- Understand, integrate, and prescribe solutions that use data modeling.
- Experienced statistical programmers using languages like and tools such as SAS, SQL, R, SPSS, Python, and Knime.

Database Management
- Define and align database requirements for various forms of data, coded and not coded, using tools like Teradata, Oracle, and Hadoop.
- Design and structure databases using tools like Teradata, Oracle, and Hadoop.

Where Do They Work?

extracted_text_claude_sonnet__claude_son:
WOMEN AND CRIMINAL VICTIMIZATION

Violence against women is an important issue that impacts individuals, families and society and which remains a barrier to gender equality in Canada and around the world.

1. 2014: OVER 1.2 MILLION VIOLENT VICTIMIZATION INCIDENTS 
self-reported* by women
Violent victimizations include sexual assault, robbery and physical assault.
*this represents 56% of all violent incidents

2. 2015: 88.2% OF SEXUAL ASSAULT VICTIMS ARE FEMALE

3. RISK FACTORS
Violence can affect anyone, but some factors can put individuals at higher risk of being victimized.

4. YOUNG WOMEN
aged 15 to 24
Violent victimization was the highest among young women in this age category, with 215 incidents per 1,000 population.

RATES OF VICTIMIZATION DECLINE WITH AGE
*per 1,000 population
- aged 25 to 34: 128 incidents
- aged 35 to 44: 68 incidents*
- aged 45 to 54: 88 incidents
- aged 55 to 64: 42 incidents*
- aged 64 and older: 14 incidents

5. INDIGENOUS WOMEN
experienced violence at a rate 2.7 times higher than non-Indigenous women in 2014.

1980: 9% of all female homicide victims were Indigenous
2015: 24% of all female homicide victims were Indigenous

6. LESBIAN AND BISEXUAL WOMEN
experienced violence at more than twice the rate reported by gay and bisexual men in 2014.

Gay and bisexual men: 121 incidents*
Lesbian and bisexual women: 281 incidents*
*per 1,000 population

*The two primary sources of data are police-reported crime data, and self-reported victimization. Self-reported incidents may not have come to the attention of the police and were collected through the General Social Survey on Canadian's safety.

These highlights were taken from the "Women in the Criminal Justice System" chapter of the 7th edition of Women in Canada: A Gender-based Statistical Report.

Canada [Government of Canada logo]

AMERICA'S
GUN VIOLENCE EPIDEMIC

Although gun violence in America is frequently attributed to the availability of firearms, another relevant factor is our nation's broken mental health care system. Although most people who suffer from mental health disorders are nonviolent, severe mental health disorders that go untreated can increase the risk for violence. Thus, if we hope to curb gun violence, we must de-stigmatize and treat mental health disorders.

GUN VIOLENCE IN AMERICA

2/3 OF ALL MASS KILLINGS INVOLVE GUNS.

In America, a mass killing — defined as having 4 victims, not including the killer — occurs once every 2 weeks.

There were 154 mass killings from 2006 to 2010.

THOSE INCLUDE
774 VICTIMS

OF THOSE VICTIMS,
161 WERE CHILDREN

GUNS & SUICIDE

31,000
GUN RELATED DEATHS IN THE UNITED STATES

19,000 OF THOSE ARE SELF INFLICTED

5% OF ALL SUICIDE ATTEMPTS ARE BY GUNSHOT.

85% OF THOSE ATTEMPTS RESULTED IN DEATH.

Suicide is the 11th leading cause of death in the United States and the third leading cause of death for people ages 15-24 years.

In July 2007, a nationwide report indicated that male veterans are twice as likely to die by suicide as compared to their civilian peers.

ACCESS TO GUNS
FEDERAL GUN LAWS CURRENTLY ENACTED INCLUDE:

- Prohibition of firearm sales or transfers to those suffering from mental health disorders and other issues.
- Prohibition of firearms possession in school zones, unless licensed by a person under federal law or unless the firearm is unloaded and in a locked container.
- Prohibition of possession by felons and some domestic violence offenders.
- Federal background checks on firearms purchases from licensed dealers; records of checks are generally kept.
- Some limits on who may purchase handguns and limits on interstate transfers.
- Prohibition of fully automatic weapon sales to civilians without special registration and tax stamp.

BUT GUN CONTROL IS LARGELY DICTATED BY INDIVIDUAL STATES, CREATING LARGE DISCREPANCIES BETWEEN STATES. FOR EXAMPLE, SOUTH DAKOTA HAS SOME OF THE WEAKEST GUN LAWS AND CALIFORNIA HAS SOME OF THE STRONGEST:

SOUTH DAKOTA
- No law authorizing the reporting of mental health information to the National Instant Criminal Background Check System (NICS)
- No permit required to purchase a handgun
- No requirement to register firearms
- No magazine capacity restriction
- Concealed carry permitted and concealed carry permit reciprocity with many states

CALIFORNIA
- State laws to track and disarm people adjudicated mentally ill
- Universal background checks for most firearm purchases
- Licensing and registration requirements for handguns
- Restriction on magazine capacities
- Prohibits possession of firearms by some categories of mentally ill persons

Gun control laws are further weakened by discrepancies in regulations for licensed firearms dealers, which are stricter, and regulations for private sales, which are more lax.

MENTAL HEALTH AND GUN VIOLENCE

7 MILLION
Americans suffer from severe mental health issues, such as schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, or major depression.

The increased risk of violence for most mentally ill people is minimal, but when paired with substance abuse or left untreated, the risk is more than doubled.

HALF OF U.S. ADULTS WILL DEVELOP A MENTAL ILLNESS DURING THEIR LIFETIME.

People with severe mental illness are roughly twice as likely to commit violent acts in their lifetime.

THE FLOUNDERING MENTAL HEALTH SYSTEM

LESS THAN HALF OF AMERICANS WITH SEVERE MENTAL HEALTH ISSUES RECEIVE ADEQUATE TREATMENT.

$1.6 BILLION
in state mental health services have been cut since 2009.

These cuts led to significant reductions in hospital and community services for those with serious mental illness, including cutbacks to:
- HOUSING
- OUTPATIENT INTERVENTIONS
- MEDICATION
- CRISIS SERVICES

13%
of children ages 8 to 15 live with severe mental health disorders

but only

20%
of those children receive treatment

ONE-HALF OF ALL LIFETIME CASES OF MENTAL ILLNESS BEGIN BY AGE 14. THEY ARE LONG DELAYS — SOMETIMES DECADES — BETWEEN THE FIRST ONSET OF SYMPTOMS AND WHEN PEOPLE SEEK AND RECEIVE TREATMENT.

500,000:
TOTAL BEDS AVAILABLE IN AMERICAN MENTAL INSTITUTIONS AT PEAK LEVELS IN THE 1950s

43,000:
TOTAL BEDS CURRENTLY AVAILABLE

This shortage of inpatient psychiatric care makes finding appropriate care difficult, if not impossible.

IN 2011
24 STATES
SLASHED MENTAL HEALTH PROVIDER REIMBURSEMENT RATES FOR MEDICAID — THE MOST IMPORTANT SOURCE OF FUNDING OF PUBLIC MENTAL HEALTH SERVICES.

REAL
SOLUTIONS

"We must not only tackle gun control but we must also fix deficiencies in the U.S. mental health system, gaps that can leave many people with serious mental illnesses and family members without an easy way to get the treatment and support we know can help avert catastrophes."

Lynn R. Goldman, pediatrician and dean of the George Washington University School of Public Health and Health Services

New Assault Weapons Ban
Senator Dianne Feinstein has reintroduced the Assult Weapons Ban and large-capacity magazines.

Funding Mental Health Care
The recently passed Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act will expand Medicaid coverage to millions of uninsured Americans and includes requirements to cover mental health care.

Gun Violence Research
On January 16, President Obama signed an executive order directing the CDC and other federal agencies to research causes of gun violence and prevention strategies.

Destigmatizing Mental Illness
On February 4, Department of Health & Human Services Secretary Kathleen Sebelius announced initiatives to reduce stigma and expand access to mental health services, including improved parity laws and outreach to keep people from seeking care.

The explosion in digital data, bandwidth and processing power — combined with new tools for analyzing the data — has sparked massive interest in the emerging field of data science. A brief history of printing

WOODBLOCKS
Stamps carved into blocks of wood.
~200

How To Use Woodblocks

1
2
3

PRINTING PRESS
~1440
The Gutenberg machine - A European innovation

For the first time in history the Bible was printed.

A Gutenberg Press with these core components...

Moveable Type
Composing Stick
meant no more hand copying

LITHOGRAPHY
1796
Based on the immiscibility of oil and water, it leaves your fingers greasy.

A low-cost, fault-tolerant method

(illustrations of flasks and stones)

The press can be temperamental
Stones are heavy and fragile
Alois Senefelder

ROTARY PRESS
1842
the longest, fastest, biggest and newest printing machine to date

The first newspaper to use it was The Baltimore Sun.

OFFSET PRINTING
1875
Colours are separated and then 'offset' - the quality achievable with this process is outstanding.

Colours are separated into Cyan, Magenta, Yellow and Black

(icons showing rollers and colour separations)

The first way to 'compose' type for photographs and complex layouts

SCREEN PRINTING
1910
One of the cheapest printing techniques, it could transfer a design onto virtually anything.

Screen printing is fairly easy, here is what you need: (2)

A basic design
A silk screen mask
Waterbased ink
Squeegee

XEROGRAPHY
1938
copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy...

INKJET PRINTING
1951
The computer can be in and do the first digital printers

Electricity (a power supply)
Ink Cartridges (for your colours)
Ink Tanks (becoming available towards the end of the century)
A computer (No need to be connected to a mainframe)

LASER PRINTING
1969
- light beam that 'impresses'

What's going on inside your printer?

Negative charge from the corona wire (or roller) attracts toner to the drum
A laser burns the image onto the drum and 'exposes' the areas to be printed
Toner is transferred to the paper
The drum and rollers then fuse the toner onto the paper using heat and pressure

3D PRINTING
1984
The design exits the boundaries of the canvas!

It uses coloured plastic filaments as ink. (2)

Many manufacturers now include 3D printers for prototyping and bespoke manufacture.
This puts ideas, objects and designs directly into the hands of makers.

FUTURE TECHNOLOGIES
2117
How will print continue beyond paper?

some plausible future printers

The Perfume Printer
(print smells onto paper?)

The Emotive Printer
(prints high-resolution mood/scent/depth?)

The Pasta Shaper
(prints food into new shapes & textures?)

Title: The Power of Empowerment
Subtitle: Women and Finances
With Northwestern Mutual

Forbes Insights and Northwestern Mutual asked women about what they want to achieve, how confident they are in attaining those goals and what makes them feel empowered. Here's what they said:

Women Are Challenged by Financial Goals
ATTAINABLE GOALS
Percentage of respondents who say they are very likely to achieve these goals:
- Having close, fulfilling relationships: 75%
- Raising happy children: 71%
- Pursuing social values: 71%
- Achieving money/wealth: 27%
- Being able to leave wealth for future generations: 26%

Reaching Financial Goals:
46% of women who are highly disciplined financial planners say they are confident they will achieve wealth vs. 15% of women who are informal planners or have not established financial goals.

Women Are Confident When They Have Financial Knowledge

WHO IS CONFIDENT ABOUT HAVING WEALTH?
Percentage who say they are confident
Level of Knowledge:
- Novice: 12%
- Intermediate: 24%
- Advanced/Expert: 45%

Working with a Financial Advisor:
60% of women who work with a financial advisor say they're confident about reaching retirement goals vs. 34% who don't have a financial advisor.

Financial Knowledge Empowers Women:
53% of financially savvy women feel empowered by participating in family finances vs. 31% of financial novices.

Women Are Empowered by Financial Stability

I FEEL EMPOWERED WHEN:
- I make personal choices to live my life the way I want: 63%
- I am comfortable about my financial situation: 60%
- I participate in my family's financial decision-making: 42%
- I influence others: 36%
- I advance in my career: 35%

Quotes included from various professionals:
"Finances are important, but it's easy to shove them to the back burner. I don't save a ton of money, but I'm saving more than in the past now that I'm older and the clock is ticking."

"We see our clients become more confident when they identify their goals and put a plan into action to achieve them."

"I see a correlation between having financial freedom and a greater sense of security in general. It allows me to live my life the way I want to and to pursue the opportunities I want."

Source: Based on a Forbes Insights and Northwestern Mutual survey of 807 women in the United States. Forbes Insights conducts primary research designed to support content marketing and include decisions for business executives.

Northwestern Mutual is the marketing name for The Northwestern Mutual Life Insurance Company (NM), Milwaukee, WI, and its subsidiaries.

ILLUSTRATIONS BY ANTHONY MUCA

- GEOGRAPHIC FOCUS
- SPECIFIC ON HOW $ IS USED
- DESCRIBES PROBLEM BEING SOLVED

DONORS GAVE ALMOST THREE TIMES MORE TO THE TANGIBLE CHARITY

ON TANGIBILITY

Intangible Project

ON GOAL PROXIMITY:

FIELD EXPERIMENTS IN DISASTER RELIEF

In this experiment, several thousand donors were sent one of four mailings that informed the donor about a fund they could support. One group was told nothing of the progress towards the goal, another that they were at 10%, another at 66% and another at 85%.

10%      66%      85%

MORE BENEFIT
Donation rates increased when told that the fund was at 85% of the goal.

LESS BENEFIT
There was no benefit in mentioning the fund was at 10% or 66% of the goal.

SOME TIPS

CREATE REAL TANGIBLE PROJECTS FOR DONORS TO FUND
ENCOURAGE DONORS TO SHARE THEIR DONATION (AND AMOUNT) IN PUBLIC
BE SPECIFIC IN HOW DONATIONS WILL BE USED TO MAKE AN IMPACT
BUILD TOWARDS YOUR GOAL BEFORE ASKING THE PUBLIC FOR DONATIONS
SHARE STORIES OF OTHER DONORS' GENEROSITY BEFORE ASKING

DONATION RATES INCREASED WHEN TOLD THAT THE FUND WAS AT 85% OF THE GOAL

IMPACT OF COVID-19 ON BUSINESSES IN CANADA
Results from the Canadian Survey on Business Conditions

WORKING REMOTELY BEFORE, DURING AND AFTER THE PANDEMIC
Proportion of businesses that reported that 10% or more of their workforce was working remotely:
Prior to February 1, 2020: 16.6%
On May 29, 2020: 32.6%
22.5% of businesses expect that 10% or more of their workforce will continue to work remotely once the pandemic is over.

THE MAJORITY OF BUSINESSES PLAN TO IMPLEMENT MEASURES TO PROTECT EMPLOYEES AND CUSTOMERS ONCE ONSITE WORK RESUMES
- 79.0% said they were likely or very likely to provide employees with face masks, gloves and other personal protective equipment
- 91.2% said they were likely or very likely to insist that employees who show any signs of illness stay home
- 87.0% said they were likely or very likely to increase sanitization in the workplace

80.5% OF BUSINESSES NEED OR EXPECT TO NEED PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT AS PHYSICAL DISTANCING MEASURES ARE RELAXED

TOP SECTORS IN NEED:
- Health care and social assistance (93.9%)
- Accommodation and food services (92.9%)
- Retail trade (91.9%)
- Transportation and warehousing (90.3%)

TOP NEEDS:
- Masks and eye protection (68.1%)
- Cleaning products (64.4%)
- Gloves (56.8%)

HOW ARE BUSINESSES IN CANADA GETTING THROUGH THE COVID-19 PANDEMIC?

BUSINESS SURVIVAL MODE:
- 63.7% were approved for funding from government programs or credit from external providers
- 23.6% that make rent or mortgage payments had their payments deferred
- 39.9% reduced staff hours or shifts
- 28.4% laid off staff
- 19.3% of businesses reported that they can continue to operate at their current level of revenue and expenditures for less than 6 months before considering further staffing actions, closure or bankruptcy.

Source: Statistics Canada, May 2020, Canadian Survey on Business Conditions: Impact of COVID-19 on businesses in Canada.

This makes it the audio media format with the least amount of lifespan with regular use.
extracted_text_claude_sonnet__claude_son:
THE LIFESPAN OF STORAGE MEDIA

Top Header Text:
For as long as we have been inventing ways to capture and present data, we have been developing media on which to store it. With each new innovation, we've seen greater convenience, increased storage capacity, and longer lifespans. But at some point, even with the utmost care, media fails... leaving us with a useless piece of plastic, tape, or metal.

The Cloud description:
The Cloud, with its extensive network of server farms, leverages redundant storage media, ensuring that in the event of hardware failure, the data is stored safely in another part of the cloud.

Icons/Indicators:
FAST | REDUNDANT | RELIABLE | ACCESSIBLE ANYWHERE | FOREVER

Storage Media Timeline (Years of Use):
- THE CLOUD: Extended blue bar
- ZIP DISK (1994): 2-10 years
- 5.25" FLOPPY DISK (1976): 2-30 years
- 3.5" FLOPPY DISK (1982): 2-15 years
- CD-RW (1997): 3-100 years
- DIGITAL TAPE (1987): 10-30 years
- DVD-R (1999): 5-100 years
- HARD DRIVE (1956): 5-75 years
- SOLID STATE HARD DRIVE (1999): 5-100+ years
- COMPACT DISC (1982): 3-100 years
- REEL AUDIO TAPE (1980s): 10-20 years
- CASSETTE AUDIO (1963): 10-20 years
- EIGHT TRACK (1964): 2-30 years
- VINYL RECORD (1981): 15-50+ years
- MINI DISC (1992): 10-15 years
- VHS TAPE (1995): 10-20 years
- DVD (1999): 30-100 years
- SUPER 8 FILM (1965): 70-100 years
- UNDEVELOPED FILM (1889): 3-6 years
- DIGITAL PHOTOS (1990s): 30-50 years
- PHOTOS (1944): 50-75 years
- MEMORY CARD (1999): 5-115 years

Additional Ways Media Can Fail:
- COMPATIBILITY
- CHEMICAL BREAKDOWN
- MAGNETISM
- DRY OUT
- PHYSICAL DAMAGE
- OXIDATION

- "CLICK OF DEATH"

A 4°C WARMER WORLD
We Can and Must Avoid It

Climate change is happening — now. Turn Down the Heat, a report on the latest climate science prepared for the World Bank, notes we’re on a path to a 4°C warmer world by the end of this century. The social, environmental, and economic consequences could be devastating. Every region of the world would be affected, but the poor and most vulnerable will suffer the most. This is not inevitable, however — with sustained policy action and climate-smart investments, warming can still be held below 2°C.

Human impact on the climate system is unequivocal.

Ice Age
Since the last Ice Age to pre-industrial times (thousands of years ago):
-7°C

Pre-industrial Times (around 1750)
The global mean temperature rose by 4-5°C.

The same warming is taking place from pre-industrial times to 2100 (only about 300 years).

Pre-industrial Times (around 1750) → Today
Sea levels have risen 15–20 cm.
The global mean temperature is 0.8°C higher.
Ice sheets in both Greenland and the Antarctic are losing mass at an accelerating rate.
Since 2002, 13 extreme weather events like floods, heatwaves, and hurricanes have been linked to man-made change.

Today → Future
If present emission trends continue, the world is on a path to a 4°C warmer world.
Likely sea level rise: 0.5–1 meter.
By 2100:
4° warmer

What could a 4°C warmer world look like?
Warming is likely to have more severe impacts on the tropics.

There would be larger impacts on agriculture and ecosystems.

Drought and aridity would likely increase in tropical developing countries.

Flooding and rising sea-level hotspots.

Sea level rise in the tropics is likely to be 15–20% higher than the global mean.

Corals Driven to Extinction
Current
0.8°C warmer — Threatened with increased bleaching
2°C warmer — Growth slows down
3°C warmer — Begin to dissolve
4°C warmer — Extinction possible

Amazon Rainforest Dieback
Current
0.8°C warmer — Home to rich biodiversity
2°C warmer — Forest fires could double
3°C warmer — Large sections could transform to savannah
4°C warmer — Large sections transform to low biomass woodlands

Water Resources Threatened
2°C warmer — Mean annual runoff decreases by 20–40% in the Danube, Mississippi, Amazon, and Murray-Darling Basins.
4°C warmer — All above changes likely to double

What can we do?
The steps we take today can help countries transition to low carbon and climate resilient growth paths for the future.

WE MUST:
- Make economic growth green and inclusive
- Scale up affordable technology solutions
- Invest in climate-smart infrastructure

Increasing finances to fight climate change

Look into the eyes of the person being toasted and say skål

Using
DISINFECTING
WIPES

at home & school

Types of Antimicrobials

Sanitizers
Reduce bacterial numbers on surfaces to a safe level.

Disinfectants
Kill bacteria, fungi, and some viruses on surfaces.

Antiseptics/Drugs
Antiseptics like hand sanitizers treat or prevent diseases on living things. They are not pesticides.

Microbes
Bacteria
Fungi
Viruses
Spores*
*Most wipes do not kill spores

What is dwell time?
-The contact time (seconds or minutes) the product needs to stay on a surface to kill microbes
-The surface should be visibly wet for the entire dwell time
-Time may change based on the microbe and purpose (sanitize vs. disinfect)

Where can they be used?
-Specific locations listed only on the label
-Use sites and dwell time vary by location and purpose

Household surfaces
Medical
Public Health

What are they?
- Antimicrobials are intended to kill or slow the growth of microbes
-Disinfectant wipes are disposable for hard surfaces like counters and tables
-Surface cleaners like wipes are not intended for use on skin
- Disinfectant wipes may LOOK similar to hand wipes. Read the label! Look for phrases like "disinfecting", "antibacterial", or "kills germs, viruses, and bacteria". When in doubt, call NPIC: 800-858-7378

Use in schools and daycares
-Only adults should use cleaning wipes – children should NOT use wipes
-Keep out of reach of children
-Follow the dwell time listed on the label
-Leave wipes in the original container
-Use the right product for each situation
-Parents: ask how children are involved in classroom cleaning

These wipes are NOT for:
Food contact
Produce
Hand wipes
Baby wipes

What is antimicrobial resistance?
If similar products are used repeatedly, they may not be as effective over time. This resistance can occur naturally, but overuse or misuse of antimicrobial products increases the chances. Follow the label and dwell time to reduce the risk of resistance.

How do I minimize risk?
-Always follow the label, including use sites and dwell times
-Never mix antimicrobial or cleaning products
-You can wear gloves if you prefer, always check the label first
-Wash hands after use
-Avoid touching wet surfaces after use
-Follow first aid instructions
-Open windows or use fans
-Store wipes out of reach of children
-Removing dirt and food from surfaces before helps wipes work
-Misuse or overuse of wipes can lead to antimicrobial resistance

Where can I get more information?
npic.orst.edu
npic@ace.orst.edu
800.858.7378

Sources
Antimicrobials Topic Fact Sheet - NPIC
Daycare and School Poison Safety - NPIC and AAPCC

EMAIL MARKETING

VS

SOCIAL MEDIA

There is a heated debate inside marketing circles right now about whether email marketing or social media is better.

While there are many valid and profitable reasons for adding social media marketing (SMM) to your toolbox,
the current research shows that it would be a costly mistake to toss email marketing out just yet.

EMAIL MARKETING  VS  SOCIAL MEDIA

Social Media Marketing

HAS NOT SURPASSED
email marketing by a long shot.

When someone 'likes' a Facebook post or follows a company on Twitter, the context is typically

MORE INDIRECT AND LESS OVERTLY COMMERCIAL.

For all the allure of SMM,
the bottom line is that

EMAIL IS STILL CONVERTING BETTER
for a variety of reasons

Social media is more geared toward
SHARING STATUS

UPDATES WITH FRIENDS
and engaging the way one would
around a water cooler or dinner party.

Research shows that spending on email marketing campaigns

INCREASED BY 60% IN 2012,
which resulted in a 4,000% return on investment!

To be effective in social media marketing, it is generally agreed that a less direct,

MORE ENTERTAINING APPROACH
that respects the nature of why people visit sites like Facebook works the best.

When someone opts in to an email list it is typically in the context of an interest

TO LEARN MORE ABOUT PRODUCT OR SERVICE.
As a result, the relationship with an email subscriber can have a commercial intent from the start.

Think of your email list as your traffic on demand source,

whenever you want to send out a campaign they are
JUST A MOUSE CLICK AWAY.

PEOPLE ON FACEBOOK LIKE TO STAY ON FACEBOOK.

so try sending them to a fan page instead of your home page.

Social media channels are

MORE COMPLEMENTARY
and additive to email than displacing.

Most social media can now run

LASER TARGETED
AD CAMPAIGNS
to people who are ideal for your product or services

Capture the people who are interested in your Facebook ads to an email list by

INCORPORATING AN OPT-IN FORM INSIDE YOUR CAMPAIGN.

The key is to recognize that both

EMAIL & SOCIAL MEDIA

are distinct and different marketing channels that

CAN BOTH BE EFFECTIVE

for your business if you design your campaigns to leverage the strengths of each.

THE BRUTAL ORIGINS

— The tradition of Christmas is highly regarded as a celebration of the birth of Jesus Christ. Although popular belief states that Jesus was born on December 25th, The New Testament gives no specific date or year of his birth.
— Roman pagans first introduced the holiday of "Saturnalia" which was a week-long period of lawlessness celebrated between December 17 - 25. During this period, Roman courts were closed, and Roman authorities stated that no one could be punished for breaking many of Rome's common laws.
— The festival began when Roman authorities chose a random victim to represent "The Lord of Misrule." At the end of the festival on December 25th, the innocent man or woman would be brutally murdered. By doing so, Romans believed they were destroying the "forces of darkness".
— In the 4th century C.E., Christianity adopted the "Saturnalia" festival in attempts to convert large amounts of people to Christianity. The problem was - there was nothing intrinsically Christian about the festival. To fix this, Christian leaders claimed Saturnalia's final day, December 25th, was the official birth date of Jesus Christ.

...and Christmas was born.

The tradition of Christmas is highly regarded as a celebration of the birth of Jesus Christ. Although popular belief states that Jesus was born on December 25th, The New Testament does not specify a date or year of his birth.

extracted_text_claude_sonnet__claude_son:
Title: Social Media Guidelines for Students and Job Seekers

Tagline: Social media plays a big role in young people's lives. But how much of a role should social media play when it comes to educational or professional relationships?

Quick Stats:
- More than 75% of U.S. college students and young professionals check their Facebook page at least once a day
- 36% of young employees aged 18-29 list an employer affiliation on Facebook
- 16: The average number of colleagues young employees are friends with on Facebook

Graph: Young Professionals in the U.S. Who Have Friended or Followed a Manager or Coworker
- Neither colleagues or managers: 35%
- Only colleagues at work: 40%
- Only their manager at work: 46%
- Both managers and colleagues: 24%

Who Should You Friend?
Categories with Pros and Cons:

Classmates:
Pros:
- It's a great way to make friends and connect outside of class
- You can connect with others for homework help
Cons:
- Some students may not add you, but don't take this personally

Faculty:
Pros:
- Some faculty use social networks to expand on classroom discussions or provide homework help
- Friending a teacher could lead to better learning and educational opportunities
Cons:
- Some view teacher-student interactions on social media as inappropriate

Coworkers:
Pros:
- Can help build inter-office relationships
Cons:
- Connecting online may make balancing personal life and professional life difficult
- May lead to gossip and other in-office problems

Competitors:
Pros:
- Friending competitors can show that you support and appreciate their accomplishments
Cons:
- Your competitor may learn company secrets from you
- Your current employer may think you want to leave

More Options:
- If you receive a friend request from a superior and want to accept, first clean up your profile and limit what he can see
- You may want to wait on friending a superior until they decide to friend you first
- If you receive a friend request from a superior and don't want to accept it, either ignore it or politely explain that you try to keep your personal and professional lives separate
- Remember to never post workplace complaints online
- Control who can see your posts and information by customizing privacy settings
- Don't make it personal if someone decides not to friend you back
- Don't friend coworkers or associates you don't trust

Sources: cisco.com | moneyland.time.com | hrcommunication.com | mainstreet.com | education.com | kavarico.com | cbsnews.com | careerookie.com | youtube.com | wikinews.com | huffingtonpost.com | abc2news.com
Information provided by: http://www.onlinedegrees.org/

question: Which market crash saw speediest recovery ?

This is significantly faster than the other crashes, with the next fastest being the coronavirus crash at 693 days, followed by the Great Recession (1,379 days), the dot-com crash (1,803 days), and the Great Depression which took the longest at 6,249 days to recover.

Text: In February 2020, the Covid-19 crisis caused the S&P 500 to decline by nearly 35%.

HOW DOES A TOUCHSCREEN PHONE WORK?
TECHNOLOGY

There are three types of technologies widely used in touch screen phones today: resistive, capacitive, and infrared.

PHONES:
- Resistive: Touch Diamond, LG Dare
- Capacitive: Samsung Messenger Touch, Samsung Instinct, HTC Blackberry Storm
- Infrared: Samsung U600 (heat), Neonode N2 (optical)

HOW IT WORKS:
- Resistive: When the top flexible layer is pressed onto the lower layer, the location of impact is calculated
- Capacitive: The current is broadcast from the corners of the screen. When a finger touches the screen, a grid of invisible beams just above the screen detects where it's touched and location is calculated
- Infrared: Heat sensitive must be touched with a warm object to register point of touch

COST TO MAKE:
Resistive: $ (lowest)
Capacitive: $$ (medium)
Infrared: $$$ (highest)

SCREEN MATERIAL:
- Resistive: Layer of flexible material (usually polyester film) over a layer of glass
- Capacitive: Glass
- Infrared: Glass

TOOL FOR INPUT:
- Resistive: Bare fingers, gloved fingers, or a stylus
- Capacitive: Bare fingers only
- Infrared: Heat-sensitive warm, bare fingers or a stylus

OUTDOOR VISIBILITY:
- Resistive: Poor visibility
- Capacitive: Good visibility, but strong ambient light impacts productivity
- Infrared: Good visibility

MULTI-TOUCH CAPABILITIES:
- Resistive: No
- Capacitive: Yes
- Infrared: Yes

DURABILITY:
- Resistive: Fair Durability. Screens are vulnerable to scratches and clear screen will wear over time and need replacing.
- Capacitive: Good Durability. Glass shatters upon major impact only.
- Infrared: Good Durability. Glass shatters upon major impact only.

SOURCES:
http://www.pocketlinks.com/mt/2010/03/the-touchscreen-technology-in-phones-article-s_3947_6_2.html
http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/28182290

EMPLOYEE SOCIAL MEDIA USAGE AT WORK
Statistics

How much of work time taken up per person in an average working day?

none 32.8%
1-15 mins 18.7%
15-30 mins 17.2%
30-60 mins 12.3%
60-120 mins 9.6%
+120 mins 9.4%

This equates to a cost per employee per year in wasted wages of:

None 32.8% — none
1-15 mins 18.7% — up to £1,019.50
15-30 mins 17.2% — up to £2,039
30-60 mins 12.3% — up to £4,078
60-120 mins 9.6% — up to £8,156
+120 mins 9.4% — over £8,156

When employees use social media at work

40% intermittently throughout the day
11.6% during toilet breaks
4.5% other
41.4% during lunch
22.3% at the end of the day
6.4% when first arriving at the office

Why employees use social media at work

34% use social media while at work to take a mental break from their job
27% to connect with friends and family while at work
24% to make or support professional connections
17% to build or strengthen personal relationships with coworkers
20% to get information that helps them solve problems at work
17% to learn about someone they work with
12% to ask work-related questions of people outside their organisation
12% to ask such questions of people inside their organisation

Who is using it most:

25 mins women
39 mins men

Employers with and without social media policies

39% with
61% without

Impact of having a social media policy

Workers whose companies have an at-work social media policy say they use social media while on the job to:

30% take a break from work, compared with 40% of workers whose employers do not have such policies.
20% stay connected to family and friends while on the job, compared with 35% of workers whose social media use is not regulated at work.
Only 16% get information that’s helpful to their job, compared with 25% of those whose workplaces have no such regulations.

How employees view their use of social media

56% believe social media ultimately helps their job performance
22% believe that it mostly hurts
16% feel that it doesn’t have much impact either way
6% see both the benefits and the drawbacks

The Cost of Employee Social Media

Averaged this is a cost of £2,592.60 per employee per year.

Multiplied by 30,645,000 employees in the UK, this is an annual cost to employers of
£79,450,227,000 i.e. nearly £80 billion.

Factoring in lost productivity, this would cost even more.
8.13% of the working week is being lost on average.
8.13% of GDP is £185,884,320,000 i.e. nearly £186 billion in GDP lost every year to the UK.

30,645,000 people employed in the UK in 2016.
£2,286,400,000,000 GDP for UK in 2015.

Source/notes:
• The cost per employee per year is based on a 2012 government report (NICE’s Workplace Health report - Local government briefing). This is a conservative estimate based on median salary scales. Wages used are an average of public and private sector pay.
• Multiplied by the number of people employed in the UK (30,645,000) to give an annual cost to employers.
• Factoring lost productivity against UK GDP gives an estimated GDP lost per year.

Sources
http://www.hse.gov.uk
http://www.ons.gov.uk
http://www.nice.org.uk

A 4°C WARMER WORLD
We Can and Must Avoid It

Climate change is happening — now. Turn Down the Heat, a report on the latest climate science prepared for the World Bank, notes we're on a path to a 4°C Celsius warmer world by the end of this century. The social, environmental, and economic consequences could be devastating. Every region of the world would be affected, but the poor and most vulnerable will suffer the most. This is not inevitable, however — with sustained policy action and climate-smart solutions, warming can still be held below +2°C.

Human impact on the climate system is unequivocal.

Ice Age → Pre‑industrial Times (around 1750)

Since the last Ice Age to pre‑industrial times (thousands of years ago):
Sea levels have risen 15–120 cm.

The global mean temperature rose by 4–5°C.

The same warming is taking place from pre‑industrial times to 2100 (only about 300 years).

Pre‑industrial Times (around 1750) → Today

Sea levels have risen 15–20 cm.

The global mean temperature is ~0.8°C higher.

Ice sheets in both Greenland and the Arctic are losing mass at an accelerating rate.

Since 2002, 13 extreme weather events like floods, heatwaves, and hurricanes have been linked to man‑made change.

Today → Future

If present emission trends continue, the world is on a path to a 4°C warmer world.

Likely sea level rise: 0.5–1 meter

By 2100:
4° warmer

Warming is likely to have more severe impacts on the tropics.

There would be longer impacts on agriculture and ecosystems.

Drought and aridity would likely increase in tropical developing countries.

Flooding and rising sea‑level hotspots

Low

- Nonprofits

Medium

Link clicks, word-of-mouth awareness, event registrations

Low

- B2B Companies

WHAT IS A
SOCIAL MEDIA
INFLUENCER?

Traditional TV and radio ads are being edged out by a younger, fresher digital option: influencer marketing.

Like
Influencer marketing pairs social media influencers, who have large trusted audiences on Facebook, Twitter, Instagram or Pinterest, with businesses looking to promote their products to new and existing customers through the influencer's channels.

This effective advertising option is taking off. Research shows 54% of companies have a stand-alone budget for sponsored social for their brand.

To understand more about social media influencers, check out the infographic below.

WHAT IS A
SOCIAL MEDIA INFLUENCER?

WHO THEY ARE
Social media icons with a great following and credibility within a certain niche. They could be:

YouTube Users
Bloggers
Celebrities
Industry Experts
Activists
Enthusiasts

WHAT THEY DO
Collaborate with businesses to create campaigns that increase brand awareness and drive sales by promoting a brand or product to their social audience.

CREATE
Value-added content about a product or service to share with their blog readers and/or social channels

POST
Product reviews, contests, giveaways, coupons, personal stories about products or services, videos, and more

INFLUENCE
Share content across multiple channels to increase awareness of the brand, product, or service

HOW INFLUENCERS MAKE MONEY
Businesses compensate influencers monetarily and/or with free samples of a product.

WHAT TO LOOK FOR IN AN INFLUENCER

HIGH ENGAGEMENT
You want influencers that are not only active online, but work to gain the trust of their followers

HIGH QUALITY CONTENT
An influencer should produce and share top-notch content, not run-of-the-mill articles that you can find anywhere on the Internet

MATCH YOUR BRAND
You want an influencer that matches your brand's tone, style and mission

TERMS YOU SHOULD KNOW
Collaborate with businesses to create campaigns that increase brand awareness and drive sales by promoting a brand or product to their social audience

SPONSORED BLOG POST
A post written by an influencer to promote a business

SOCIAL SPONSORSHIP
Posts on an influencer's social channels to promote a business

INFLUENCER MARKETPLACE
Trusted source that connects businesses with influencers, & helps create a campaign

INFLUENCERS AGENCY
A company that helps businesses promote their brand through influencers

BE AWARE.
COVID-19 is a new respiratory illness that was first discovered in Wuhan, China. It is transmitted from person to person.

- Runny nose
- Cough 
- Sore throat

- Pneumonia
- Severe respiratory disease

- People over age 60

- Through close contact with an infected person

- By touching contaminated objects or surfaces and then touching your mouth, nose or eyes

Serious cases may require oxygen and ventilatory support.

PREPARE.
- Make sure you get your information from a reliable source.
- Wash your hands regularly with soap and water or use an alcohol-based gel.

ACT.
- Avoid close contact with people who have flu-like symptoms.
- Avoid touching your eyes, nose or mouth with unwashed hands.
- Cover your mouth with the inside of your elbow when you cough or sneeze or use a tissue and dispose of used tissue immediately and wash your hands.

Note: Information may change when more is known about the disease.
February 2020.

question: which are the touch screen technologies that uses glass for screen?
prediction_claude_sonnet__claude_sonnet: According to the document, both Capacitive and Infrared touchscreen technologies use glass for their screen material, while Resistive technology uses a layer of flexible material (usually polyester film) over a layer of glass.
extracted_text_claude_sonnet__claude_son:
HOW DOES A TOUCHSCREEN PHONE WORK?
TECHNOLOGY

There are three types of technologies widely used in touch screen phones today: resistive, capacitive, and infrared.

PHONES:
- Resistive: Touch Diamond, LG Dare
- Capacitive: Samsung Messager Touch, Samsung Instinct, HTC Blackberry Storm
- Infrared: Samsung U600 (heat), Nintendo N2 (optical)

HOW IT WORKS:
- Resistive: When the top flexible layer is pressed into the lower layer, location of impact is calculated
- Capacitive: The current is broadcast from the corners of the screen. When a finger touches the screen, location is calculated based on the time delays
- Infrared: A grid of invisible beams just above the screen. Point of contact disrupts beam

COST TO MAKE:
Resistive: $
Capacitive: $$
Infrared: $$$

SCREEN MATERIAL:
- Resistive: Layer of flexible material (usually polyester film) over a layer of glass
- Capacitive: Glass
- Infrared: Glass

TOOL FOR INPUT:
- Resistive: Bare fingers, gloved fingers, or stylus
- Capacitive: Bare fingers only
- Infrared: Heat-sensitive warm, bare fingers or a stylus

OUTDOOR VISIBILITY:
- Resistive: Poor visibility
- Capacitive: Good visibility but strong ambient light impacts productivity
- Infrared: Good visibility

MULTI-TOUCH CAPABILITIES:
- Resistive: No
- Capacitive: Yes
- Infrared: Yes

DURABILITY:
- Resistive: Fair Durability. Screens are vulnerable to scratches and slow screen response time will wear over time and need replacing.
- Capacitive: Good Durability. Glass shatters upon major impact only.
- Infrared: Good Durability. Glass shatters upon major impact only.

SOURCES:
http://www.pocketables.com/2010/04/how-touch-screen-technology-in-phones-works-s.html/&_2.html
http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/28182290

highest scoring wicket-keeper

He is the highest scoring wicket-keeper captain on the world in a Test. He's the only wicket keeper in the world who has taken a wicket

COMCAST BUSINESS
Proud Partner of Team USA

As Team USA is preparing for the Olympic Winter Games 2018, it is important to realize how The Games have changed over the years for Team USA. From how they trained for the Olympic Winter Games 1924 – the first Olympic Winter Games – to how we watched and shared Team USA’s success – it’s a whole new world, a world driven by technology.

Explore our timeline. You’ll find facts and comparisons of how Team USA and The Games have changed through the years and get a glimpse into the technology behind them.

Welcome to:
Team USA at the Olympic Winter Games:

THEN & NOW

ENHANCED TRAINING WITH TECHNOLOGY FOR TEAM USA

1924

Early Days for Team USA at the Olympic Winter Games

Inability to monitor vitals or receive feedback during training
Could not learn from practice runs, which made coaching a guessing game
Difficult to access medical history and past injuries slowed down recovery times

NOW

Technology Helps Prevent Injuries and Speeds Recovery for Team USA

Skin patches monitor heart rate, body temperature and sweat composition during training

SOCIAL MEDIA
SOCIAL LIFE

HOW TEENS VIEW THEIR DIGITAL LIVES

Ninety percent of America's teens have used social media, and the majority of them believe it's a more positive than negative influence. But many say they're "addicted" to their devices and that they want to disconnect sometimes. Teens also say that talking in person is better than texting, and face-to-face conversations are more fun than communicating online. Teens tell us:

IT'S A DAILY DIGITAL DIET–
WE LOVE OUR SOCIAL AND DIGITAL MEDIA!

EVERY DAY, WE:
Text
68%

Visit social networking sites
51%

Send or receive tweets
11%

75%
OF US HAVE A PROFILE ON A SOCIAL NETWORKING SITE.
OUR MAIN SITE IS:

68%  6%  1%

23%
OF US USE AT LEAST TWO DIFFERENT TYPES OF SOCIAL MEDIA EVERYDAY

SOCIAL NETWORKING MAKES US FEEL BETTER THAN WORSE

We feel that social networking helps our relationships with friends (52%) and family (37%) vs. hurts our relationships with friends (4%) and family (2%).

And it makes us feel less shy (29%) and more confident (20%) vs. more shy (3%) and less confident (4%).

Only 5% of us believe that social networking makes us feel more depressed vs. 10% who feel less depressed.

WE PREFER FACE TIME TO SCREEN TIME

Talking in person is our favorite way to communicate because it's more fun and easier to understand what other people mean. If we can't do that, we text. Very few of us prefer social networking as our go-to way to connect, and many of us believe that using social media can interfere with face-to-face communication.

TALK TO ME!
Our favorite way to communicate is:
IN PERSON (49%)
BY TEXT (33%)
ON SOCIAL NETWORKS (7%)
BY PHONE (4%)
ON TWITTER (1%)

WE HAVE A BAD CONNECTION...
Many of us believe that using social media:
Takes away from time we can spend with others face to face (34%)
Distracts us from the people we're with (44%)
Creates frustration when our friends pay more attention to their gadgets than to us (45%)

"Moments only happen in person"
"I enjoy seeing [my friends] laugh at my jokes."
"It's boring to talk to someone that has to check Facebook every 5 minutes."

SOMETIMES WE GET TIRED OF BEING WIRED

Many of us sometimes wish that we – and the people around us – would disconnect. It's not just about being tired of technology; it turns out that many of us who talk about tuning out have encountered some derogatory digital dialogue.

36% of us sometimes wish we could go back to a time when there was no Facebook.

More than a third of us who want to unplug often encounter racist, sexist, and homophobic content.

41% of us consider ourselves "addicted" to our phones, and 20% to social sites. 43% of us sometimes wish we could "unplug," and 21% wish our parents would, too.

"Sometimes it's nice to just sit back and relax with no way possible to communicate with anyone in any way. That's why I occasionally 'lose' my cell phone."

"Technology and social networking have absorbed some of my close friendships, and they've dwindled. I miss the times where we could just go hang out and laugh."

SOCIAL MEDIA, SOCIAL LIFE: IT'S NOT GENDER NEUTRAL

Every day, more girls than teen boys text. In the daily Twitter-verse, there are also more girls than boys, and when it comes to photo posting, it's especially a girl thing...but not always a good thing.

77% TEXT DAILY
33% EVER TWEETED
75% LOVE POSTING PHOTOS

60% TEXT DAILY
22% EVER TWEETED
42% LOVE POSTING PHOTOS

For girls, especially, putting their pictures online can be stressful:
57% girls vs. 28% boys sometimes feel left out after seeing photos of others together online
45% girls vs. 24% boys worry about other people posting ugly photos of them online
28% girls vs. 9% boys have edited photos of themselves before posting

extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
7
SCIENCE-BACKED WAYS
READING
Makes You Healthy

1
Increases longevity by 23%

Study:
A 2016 study observed the reading habits of 3653 people. Study participants were classified into those who:
• Read books for 3.5 hours or more per week
• Read books for up to 3.5 hours a week
• Did not read at all

The result:
When followed up over 12 years, it was found that those who:
• Read books for more than 3.5 hours a week were 23% less likely to die when compared to those who did not read books.
• Read books for up to 3.5 hours a week were 17% less likely to die when compared to those who did not read books.

Why do book readers live longer?
Reading books provides a higher survival advantage than reading newspapers or magazines because book readers undergo "deep reading".

This means readers:
• Draw connections to other parts of the material
• Find applications to the outside world
• Ask questions about the content

Deep reading aids survival because:
1 Engaging the brain throughout your life in mentally stimulating activities such as deep reading reduces cognitive decline.
2 Deep reading promotes:
Empathy
Social perception
Emotional intelligence
3 These lead to better relationships and a good social network.

A good social network has been shown to be associated with a higher survival rate.
• Friends can provide support and a source of comfort, shielding from the harmful impacts of stress.
• Friends can also bring purpose and meaning into people’s lives.

2
Reduces stress

Reading reduces stress levels by 68%, more than any other activity.

READING 68%
LISTENING TO MUSIC 61%
DRINKING TEA OR COFFEE 54%
WALKING 42%

Just 6 minutes of reading:
• Slows your heart rate
• Eases muscle tension
• Alters your state of mind

3
Promotes relaxation and sleep

Reading can help you sleep if you make it a part of your bedtime ritual.

Why?
• It helps relax your body and mind
• It provides a signal that it is almost time to go to bed

Make sure it's a print book.
It's best to be reading a print book in the evening if you are about to go to sleep as evening use of e-readers and smartphones can disrupt your circadian rhythm and disturb sleep.

4
Staves off Alzheimer's disease, dementia and mental decline

• Readers are 2.5 times less likely to develop Alzheimer's disease.
• Frequent brain exercises, like reading, decreases mental decline in the elderly by 32%.

Why?
• Reading is mentally stimulating and helps keep the brain active.
• When you read you have to remember a lot of things, like:
Characters
Main plot
Sub-plots
• All the developments throughout a story are a new memory in your brain and therefore new neural pathways are created and existing ones strengthened.
• The brain operates very much like a muscle — you have to exercise it to keep it healthy and strong.

5
Helps with depression

Reading can be an important non-medical intervention for people with depression.

Reading group trials, conducted at a mental health day-hospital and at a GP surgery centre, for people diagnosed with depression, have been shown to reduce symptoms of depression in participants.

The participants in the reading group reported feeling more:
• Confident
• Talkative
• Willing to listen
• Willing to interact with the other group participants

6
Boosts happiness

Life experiences make you happier than material things.

A recent study showed that experiential products designed to create or enhance life experiences, like books, are total happiness boosters.

Other experiential products include:
SPORTING GOODS
VIDEO GAMES
MUSICAL INSTRUMENTS

Reading can also help fill up your fulfillment tank.
• A study found that older adults who continue to read and learn are much more satisfied with their lives.
• Another study has shown that those who read books reported better social connections and more happiness with their life.

7
Increases social connection

Reading develops theory of mind, which leads to better social connections.

Definition: Theory of Mind is the ability to ascribe mental states, such as feelings, beliefs, thoughts and goals, to yourself and to others. It is the capacity to understand that other people's feelings and beliefs may be different from your own.

Social connections increase life expectancy
• A meta-analysis done across 148 studies showed that people who have a good social network tended to live longer.
• The absence of social connections is just as bad as smoking up to 15 cigarettes a day.
• Loneliness and social isolation have been shown to be associated with early death.

Spot TV shows a decrease from 45% in Q1 to 24% in Q2, then increased to 35.5% in Q3. Spot Radio fluctuated from 6% to 13% to 11%, and Print remained constant at 1% throughout all quarters.
extracted_text_claude_sonnet__claude_son:
HOW SOCIAL MEDIA CHANGED THE AD GAME
by: @MDGadvertising

Almost every industry has been disrupted by the rise of social media. Nowhere is that more plain than in the world of advertising. Marketers have had to change nearly every aspect of their business to keep up with consumer and technological trends. Here, we take a look at some of these key changes.

CHANGED CUSTOMERS
Today, social media makes up the largest portion of how consumers spend their time online. Marketers have had to adapt online campaigns to target consumers where they devote most of their attention.

Top Online Categories by Total Internet Time:
- Social Network/Blogs: 22.5%
- Online Games: 9.8%
- Email: 7.6%
- Videos/Movies: 4.5%
- Search: 4%
- Instant Messaging: 3.3%
- Software Manufacturers: 3.2%
- Classifieds/Auctions: 2.9%
- Current Events/Global News: 2.6%
- "Other": 35.1%
(*"Other" includes multi-category entertainment, adult, Internet tools/web services, mass merchandise, computer information, sports information, research tools and more)

CHANGED PRIORITIES
Even several years into the era of Web 2.0, advertisers are still seeing increased demand for online marketing services. At the same time, nearly all other ad media are losing priority.

Ad Media Clients Are Most Focused on According to U.S. Ad Agencies:
Q1 2011:
- Spot TV: 45%
- Internet/Digital: 23%
- Spot Radio: 6%
- Print: 1%

Q2 2011:
- Internet/Digital: 41%
- Spot TV: 24%
- Spot Radio: 13%
- Print: 1%

Q3 2011:
- Internet/Digital: 35.5%
- Spot TV: 35.5%
- Spot Radio: 11%
- Print: 1%

CHANGED SPENDING
Thanks to clients' changing priorities and the ever-evolving social media landscape, CMOs at some of the top Fortune 500 companies forecast that spending will nearly double current levels in the next five years.

Social Marketing Budget Changes from 2010-2011:
- 45% Increased
- 36% Stayed the Same
- 13% N/A
- 5% Don't Know
- 1% Decreased

Planned Social Media Spending as Share of Marketing Budget According to CMOs:
- Current Social Media Spending: 7.1%
- Social Media Spending in the Next 12 Months: 10.1%
- Social Media Spending in the Next 5 Years: 17.5%

CHANGED TACTICS
Old ad models aren't sustainable on new media. With a new set of popular platforms, traditional advertisers have had to adopt new tactics.

MOST-USED TACTICS FOR ENGAGING SOCIAL FOLLOWERS:
1. Social share buttons in email or on website
2. Unique content exclusively for fans/followers
3. "Like" buttons on brand website
4. Brand videos on YouTube, Vimeo, etc.
5. Monitoring social media for brand reputation

CHANGED METRICS
Generating leads and boosting sales are no longer the top aim of marketers. Social media offers a slew of new metrics to measure marketing success.

Ways Marketers Measure Social Media Marketing Success:
- Numbers linking as friends, followers, likes: 60%
- Qualified leads from social media: 39%
- Sharing, forwarding, retweeting, or posting brand content: 35%
- Visits or time spent with branded social content: 30%
- Brand awareness/favorability: 25%
- Incremental sales attributable to social media: 18%

Sources: LIMELIGHT, STRATA, CHIEF MARKETER, AMERICAN MARKETING ASSOCIATION, DUKE FUQUA SCHOOL OF BUSINESS

mdgadvertising.com
twitter.com/mdgadvertising

Cooling Off

extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
HOW TO CREATE
BEAUTIFUL CALLIGRAPHY

YOU WILL NEED...
a ruler or set square
permanent ink
nibbed drawing pen
gouache paint
paper
sticky tape
tissues
a couple of pencils
old cloths or towels
water
a flat-edged paintbrush
chisel-tip black marker

SETTING UP
your tools should be within easy reach
tape your paper to the desk for extra stability
work on a slanted desk or a table if possible
give yourself plenty of room to work
don't keep your left elbow next to your paper! If you knock it over, you'll spoil your work
make sure you're comfortable

PRACTICE MAKES PERFECT
tape two pencils together with sticky tape
hold your pencils at a 45° angle - the tips of your pencils are like the nib of your pen
keeping the pencils steady, start making some marks, try some zig-zags or curves

FILLING YOUR PEN
never dip your pen directly into the ink pot
use your brush to gather a horizontal stripe of ink or gouache paint across the pen's nib
you're ready to start some calligraphy!

BEFORE YOU BEGIN...
put on some old clothes - calligraphy can be messy!
make sure you're sitting comfortably - try not to slouch
cover your lap with some old towels or cloths - ink can stain your clothes

PREPARING YOUR PAPER

1 DRAW YOUR BASELINE
Find this line by aligning your ruler lengthways across the top of your paper. Then, with your pencil, draw a faint line between the two dots. This is your baseline.

2 CREATE A NIB LADDER
A nib ladder is a good way to form a guide for measuring the size and height of your calligraphy letters.

3 DRAW THE X-HEIGHT
A typical x-height for lowercase letters is about 4-5 nib heights. Using your nib ladder draw the x-height across your paper to form your main guideline.

4 DRAW THE ASCENDER & DESCENDER
From your x-height, measure about 4-5 nib heights above and below to draw your ascender and descender lines.

5 DRAW THE CAP-HEIGHT
Cap-height is the height of the capitals in your calligraphy. It sits above the x-height and below the ascender line.

In the age of big data, parsing unwieldy amounts of information can lead to world-changing innovation.

Who Are They?

BUSINESS ANALYSTS
Research and extract valuable information from structured and unstructured sources to explain historical, current, and future business performance; determine the best analytical models and approaches to present and explain solutions to business users.

DATA SCIENTISTS
Design, develop, and deploy algorithms through statistical programming that support business decision-making tools; manage large amounts of data; create visualizations to aid in understanding.

Both business analysts and data scientists are experts in using data to inform decisions, but they apply their skills in different ways, using the same or similar tools. The skills listed below describe someone who has earned a master’s degree in the field.

Decision-Making
Instigate change by transforming business insights from interdisciplinary data analytics into tangible resources for business decisions.
Data Scientists: Discover the opportunity in datasets by mining data and with machine learning algorithms to support decision-making.

THE CHINESE NEW YEAR IS ALSO KNOWN AS THE
SPRING FESTIVAL & LUNAR NEW YEAR.

The date changes every year because it is based on the lunar-solar Chinese calendar.     The festival happens in winter, when people look forward to the renewal of spring.

THE CHINESE NEW YEAR,
春節, CHUN JIE or “CHUN-JYEAH”
has been observed for more than 3,000 years.

THE
YEAR OF THE PIG
OFFICIALLY BEGINS
FEBRUARY 5,
2019.

IT WILL
GIVE WAY TO THE
YEAR OF THE RAT
ON
JANUARY 25,
2020

People observing Chinese New Year often celebrate with special decorations, firecrackers and fireworks, and red envelopes filled with money.

CHINESE NEW YEAR DECORATIONS
are often red because red is considered lucky in the Chinese culture.

CHINESE NEW YEAR FOODS
are often deemed lucky based on symbolic meanings and/or if the name of a food sounds similar to other Chinese words for luck or wealth.

6 LUCKY
CHINESE NEW YEAR
FOODS

1
CITRUS FRUIT
- In Chinese the word for pomelo sounds like the word for to have.
- Tangerines, mandarins, and kumquats are said to evoke gold, so having them can bring wealth and increase prosperity.
- The word for orange or tangerine sounds similar to the Chinese words for success.
- Another way to write tangerine is comprised of the Chinese character for luck.

2
DUMPLINGS
- Chinese dumplings symbolize great wealth.
- Depending on how they are prepared, dumplings can be made to resemble silver ingots or gold bars.

3
SPRING ROLLS OR EGG ROLLS
- Similar to dumplings, egg rolls and spring rolls may resemble gold bars.
- Chinese New Year spring rolls symbolize wealth.

4
LONGEVITY NOODLES
- The length of the noodles represents long life.
- Cutting the noodles into small pieces is frowned upon, as it means the person's life is to be cut short.

5
RICE CAKE
- In Chinese legend, rice cakes would be prepared and served to a Chinese god of the kitchen, in the hopes that when the god returned to heaven before the new year, the god would speak well of the family.
- The Chinese words for these cakes sound like the words for growing or getting higher every year, so they symbolize a prosperous year ahead.
- The sweetness of the cakes also represents a sweet life.

6
WHOLE FISH
- Serving whole fish symbolizes prosperity.
- The Chinese word for fish sounds like the Chinese word for surplus. Traditionally, Chinese people believed in having a surplus of money or goods at the end of the year because having something leftover meant they could make more in the year to come.

CHINESE
NEW YEAR
DUMPLING LORE

Flat dumplings symbolize poverty, so New Year dumplings are often shaped with many pleats.

If you eat a dumpling that contains a white thread, you'll have bad luck.  (illustration)

A dumpling with a coin inside means you'll get rich.

When serving, dumplings should be arranged in lines, not circles, because a circle suggests that you won't make progress in life and will keep going in circles.

To pass on wishes of amassing wealth, when eating dumplings it's traditional to include a coin in one dumpling and the person who finds it will have good fortune.

"ZHAO CAI JIN BAO"
JAOW TSEYE JIN BAOWW
招财进宝
meaning "Bring in wealth and treasure"

Left vs Right

CORE VIEWS

Left side (Red)
- To implement universal participation in politics among citizens

- Focus on civil liberties and the protection of individual rights

Views on Democracy

Left
- Participatory Democracy
- Direct Democracy
- On the premise of equality of property and social order, universal democracy is ensured by citizen engagement
- Government should reflect popular will and prioritize equality

Right
- Representative Democracy
- Emphasizes procedural norms and protection of minority rights
- Emphasizes efficient governance, rule of law, and protection against tyranny
- Democracy should enable innovation while preserving individual freedoms

Views on Globalisation

Left

- Embrace global solidarity, international norms, and cross-border cooperation
- Cautious about neoliberal trends; advocate reforms to balance wealth and power

Right

- Regulation to ensure fair competition and curb corruption

Right

Right

Right

The first Christmas tree was the centerpiece of holiday festivities in fifteenth century Latvia. Young men and women danced and sang around the tree before setting it on fire the last night of festivities. From those early traditions, to the first American tree in 1816, and into the present Christmas season, Christmas trees have been the focal point of holiday cheer. This timeline takes a look at some of the significant moments in the modern history of this Christmas icon.

Landmark trees

Christmas culture
Tree industry

1600
First decorated tree

1883
First artificial tree
Sears, Roebuck & Company began offering the first artificial Christmas trees -- 33 limbs for $.50 and 55 limbs for $1.00.

“It’s not good to cut down trees for mere decoration,” says President Teddy Roosevelt in 1901, launching a century long debate on real vs. artificial trees.

1910
1910s Glass candleholders & tinsel trees

1920s Tree farms

1930

The West Coast reinvents Christmas with flocking kits that can be applied with vacuum cleaners.

1958

1970-80s

Meanwhile, sales continue to boom. Over 17.4 million artificial trees were sold in 2007.

2001 Lifelike polyethylene trees
