HOW TO CREATE
BEAUTIFUL
CALLIGRAPHY

YOU WILL NEED...

a ruler or set square
permanent ink
nibbed drawing pen
gouache paint
paper
sticky tape

tissues
a couple of pencils
old cloths or towels
water
a flat-edged paintbrush
chisel-tip black marker

SETTING UP

your tools should be within easy reach
tape your paper to the desk for extra stability
work on a slanted desk or a table if possible
give yourself plenty of room to work
don't keep your left wrist over the top of your paper - if you knock it over, you'll spoil your work
make sure you're comfortable

PRACTICE MAKES PERFECT

tape two pencils together with sticky tape
hold your pencils at a 45° angle - the tips of your pencils are like the nib of your pen
keeping the pencils steady, start making some marks, try some zig-zags or curves

FILLING YOUR PEN

never dip your pen directly into the ink pot
use your brush to paint a horizontal stripe of ink or gouache paint across the pen's nib
you're ready to start some calligraphy!

BEFORE YOU BEGIN...

put on some old clothes - calligraphy can be messy!
make sure you're sitting comfortably - try not to slouch
cover your lap with some old towels or cloths - ink can stain your clothes

PREPARING YOUR PAPER

adgh

1 DRAW YOUR BASELINE
Find this line by aligning your ruler lengthways across the top of your paper. Then, with your pencil, draw a line along the bottom of the ruler. This is your baseline.

2 CREATE A NIB LADDER
A nib ladder is a good way to form a guide for measuring the size and height of your calligraphy letters. Put your pen parallel to your ruler and draw a square shape the size of your pen nib on the paper. From the top of your first square, draw a second square the same size and height as your first. Continue in this way until you have formed a diagonal line of 10 squares.

3 DRAW THE X-HEIGHT
A typical x-height for lowercase letters is about 4-5 nib heights tall. Using your nib ladder, measure 4-5 nib heights up from the baseline and draw a horizontal line. This is your x-height line - keep your pen upright with your pencil.

4 DRAW THE ASCENDER & DESCENDER
From your x-height, measure 4-5 nib heights above and below and draw two more horizontal lines to mark the ascender and descender heights. These lines indicate the top and bottom limits for letters.

5 DRAW THE CAP-HEIGHT
Cap height is the height of the capitals in your calligraphy. It is usually around the ascender height. Measure using your nib ladder and draw a horizontal cap-height line.

WARNING
If you're designing your nib ladder with gouache, allow it to dry before using your pen. We recommend using a block chisel-tip marker to practice before using your nib pen until your grid gets comfortable.

friendster
THE HISTORY
of social media

Social media channels seem to be everywhere and with 135 minutes of our time spent on social media platforms every day, it's crazy to think that it's only been 20 years since the first social media site was made. Without any further ado, here we have the history of social media.

THE BEGINNING

Samuel Morse made the first step towards some resemblance of social media when he sent a telegraph from Washington D.C to Baltimore. Morse's first message read "What Have God Wrought?".

1844

1969

MILITARY'S FIRST SOCIAL MEDIA ATTEMPT

ARPAN (Advanced Research Projects Agency Network) wanted to connect Universities on a proto-internet. It worked great, if a transmitting two letters was the goal. Unfortunately, the letters 'LO' weren't the complete 'LOGIN' that they had planned to send over.

START OF SOCIAL SITES

From 1997 to 2001, Six Degrees had a peak of about 1 million members who were able to create profiles and "friend" each other.

1997

CREATIVITY AND STATUS BLOGS

LiveJournal was a platform where users could blog as a form of keeping friends updated with their lives, a format later used by Facebook for their status.

1999

A CHALLENGER EMERGES

Friendster, one of the first social media sites, is created and launched by Peter Chin, Jonathan Abrams, and Owen Lee. Within the next year they had 3 million users. Google, widely considered one of the biggest blunders of Silicon Valley.

2002

LINK UP WITH BUSINESSES

LinkedIn gave users a place to post their resume and private message people with a business-orientated mindset. It's a very popular way for businesses to recruit new employees and for users to search for jobs. By 2004 the number of users on the site would reach 1 million, 3 years later they would break 500 million users.

2003

GMAIL RECEIVED

Google creates its own email service, Gmail, in January. In the UK Gmail was branded as Google Mail, due to a trademark dispute, until 2009 when they changed the branding back after the dispute was over.

2004

FACEBOOK BEGINS

In February 2004, Facebook was launched by Mark Zuckerberg, along with his Harvard roommates. The site was initially limited to Harvard students but in the following months and years, its membership would be worldwide, reaching the number of users to around 2.2 billion in 14 years.

2004

BIRTH OF YOUTUBE

YouTube a platform on which users can upload and share videos, was made public in February 2005. The first video to hit one million views was "Me at the Zoo" and within this year, thanks to a mix of features, the site exploded. Facebook is now available in the UK, Ireland, Canada, Australia and New Zealand on October 1st.

2005

MYSPACE IS BOOMING

Myspace became the most popular social network in the US during 2006! That's just 3 years after it was founded.

Twitter is launched publically on July 15th

2006

750 TWEETS PER SECOND!

65 million tweets a day are being sent out on Twitter, that's around 750 tweets every second.

2010

LAUNCH OF SNAPCHAT

Snapchat, an image messaging app, is launched in September after being tested as an iOS only app known as Picaboo in July. One of the founding members is pushed out before Snapchat is released.

2011

SHORT VIDEOS, MEGA VIEWS

Vine, a video hosting service specialising in short videos, goes public in June. Twitter acquires Vine for $30 million in the October of that year.

2013

UK'S INSTA ADS

Instagram brings sponsored Ads to target the UK in September following their inclusion on the app in America.

2014

LIFE OF A STARMAN

NASA's #YearInSpace started, giving Twitter followers updates on Scott Kelly an astronaut who lived in space for a year!

2015

VR AND FAKE NEWS

Virtual Reality, live videos, and 360-degree videos required all video content on social media sites like Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram.
There was a crackdown on fake news throughout social media platforms like Facebook and Twitter.

2018

After all of this, we now have a beautiful array of social media channels to pick and choose from; Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, Snapchat and more. We wonder what next advancement social media creators will think up. What do you think will happen in the future world of social media?

BUSINESS ANALYTICS vs DATA SCIENCE

In the age of big data, parsing unwieldy amounts of information can lead to world-changing innovation.
To understand this data, companies are hiring a variety of specialists, including business analysts and data scientists.

Who Are They?

BUSINESS ANALYSTS
Research and extract valuable information from structured and unstructured sources to explain historical, current, and future business performance; determine the best analytical models and approaches to present and explain solutions to business users.

DATA SCIENTISTS
Design, develop, and deploy algorithms through statistical programming that support business decision-making tools; manage large amounts of data; create visualizations to aid in understanding.

What Education Do They Have?

The majority of business analysts come from a variety of backgrounds including business and humanities, while data scientists tend to come from computer science, mathematics, and technology backgrounds.

BUSINESS ANALYSTS                 DATA SCIENTISTS
30%                                28%
Bachelor's

56%                                48%
Master's

11%                                20%
Doctoral

Sample Undergraduate Majors

• Business Administration                 • Mathematics
• Information Technology                 • Computer Science
• Finance                                • Information Science
• Political Science                      • Computer Software Engineering
• Anthropology                           • Technical Communications
• Economics                              • Computer Information Systems
• History                                • Statistics
• Psychology                             •

What Skills Do They Have?

Both business analysts and data scientists are experts in using data to inform decisions, but they apply their skills in different ways, using the same or similar tools. The skills listed below describe someone who has earned a master's degree in the field.

Legend:
Blue • Business Analysts
Orange • Data Scientists

Decision-Making
• Instigate change by transforming business insights from interdisciplinary data analysis into tangible resources for business decision-making.
• Discover the opportunity in datasets by mining data and writing machine learning algorithms to support decision-making processes.

Applied Problem Solving
• Define business problems and translate statistical analysis into data-driven business intelligence that improves business performance.
• Create the foundation of analysis by which an applied business problem can be solved.

Data Analysis
• Research, interpret, and visualize raw data using predictive, prescriptive and descriptive analysis to make it useful and accessible for business users.
• Manage and organize large sets of multivariate data using methods like linear discriminant analysis and multilinear regression selection.

Analytical Models
• Understand, integrate, and prescribe solutions that use data modeling.
• Experienced statistical programmers using languages and tools such as SAS, SQL, R, SPSS, Python, and Knime.

Database Management
• Define and align database requirements for various forms of data, coded and not coded, using tools like Teradata, Oracle, and Hadoop.
• Design and structure databases using tools like Teradata, Oracle, and Hadoop.

Where Do They Work?

BUSINESS ANALYSTS
25%        Finance and insurance
10%        Computer systems design and related services
8%         Manufacturing
8%         State and local government, excluding education and hospitals
8%         Management of companies and enterprises

DATA SCIENTISTS
26%        Federal government
18%        Computer systems design and related services
13%        Colleges, universities, and professional schools
11%        Research and development in the physical, engineering, and life sciences
8%         Software publishers

Data Scientists: 26,700

Title: Covid-19 policy responses

Header: Governments around the world scrambled to mitigate the health and economic impacts of covid-19, with a wide variation in choice and timing of measures. The corresponding outbreaks in affluent countries are orders of magnitude more or less severe. The timings below indicate that the UK was slower to act than many countries, despite warnings from other regions and the World Health Organization (WHO).

Government Actions:
National:
- Schools required to close
- Gatherings limited ≤100 people
- Requirement to stay at home
- Economic support >50% of lost salary

Regional:
- Internal movement closed or mostly prohibited
- International arrivals quarantined from high risk regions
- Testing of anyone showing covid-19 symptoms
- Comprehensive contact tracing for all cases

Timeline Key Dates:
31 Dec 2019: Reports of pneumonia with unknown cause in China sent to WHO
30 Jan 2020: WHO declares "public health emergency of international concern"
11 Mar: WHO announces covid-19 as a pandemic

24 Jan: UK scientific advisory groups choose not to assess lockdown measures

extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
A HISTORY OF
AMERICAN PAPER MONEY

FROM COLONIAL BILLS TO SILVER AND GOLD CERTIFICATES TO EVEN A $100,000 NOTE, AMERICAN PAPER MONEY HAS UNDERGONE MANY CHANGES THROUGHOUT ITS HISTORY.

1781
FIRST NATIONAL BANK
The Congress charters the Bank of North America as the first bank chartered by the United States.

1775
CONTINENTAL CONGRESS
Paper money is issued by the Continental Congress to finance the Revolutionary War.

COLONIAL BILLS
The Massachusetts Bay Colony issues the first paper currency to pay for soldiers and supplies. Other colonies follow.

1785
THE U.S. DOLLAR
The dollar becomes the official monetary unit of the United States.

1791
FIRST U.S. CENTRAL BANK
Congress charters the Bank of the United States. The First Bank operates for 20 years as a central bank for the U.S. Treasury.

1792
FEDERAL MINT SYSTEM
The Coinage Act establishes the U.S. Mint and creates the U.S. dollar coin.

1816
SECOND U.S. CENTRAL BANK
Congress charters the Second Bank of the United States as a central bank for the newly formed country.

1836
FREE BANKING ERA
States chartered banks begin issuing their own banknotes. Many banks failed and private banknotes were often of dubious value.

1861
CIVIL WAR "GREENBACKS"
In an effort to finance the Civil War, the government issues demand notes and later legal tender notes, the first to use green ink on the back.

1862
FIRST $2, $5, $10 AND $100 BILLS ARE ISSUED
Legal Tender Notes are U.S. Notes. Later replaced. Notes were made from 1862 to 1971.

1865
ESTABLISHMENT OF U.S. SECRET SERVICE
The U.S. Secret Service was created as part of the Treasury Department to investigate counterfeiting and to protect the nation's currency.

1877
BUREAU OF ENGRAVING AND PRINTING
The Treasury Department's Bureau of Engraving and Printing takes over production and printing.

1878
FIRST SILVER CERTIFICATES
Silver certificates start being issued in exchange for other dollars.

1882
FIRST GOLD CERTIFICATES
Gold certificates were authorized in 1882 for use by government and banks. Used from 1882 to 1933.

1913
FEDERAL RESERVE ACT OF 1913
The Federal Reserve System is created to act as the central banking system of the United States. Federal Reserve Notes are created.

1914
LARGE SIZE FEDERAL RESERVE NOTES
$1, $2, $5, $10, $20, $50, $100, $500 and $1,000 were issued in large-size notes beginning in 1914.

1918
LARGE SIZE HIGH DENOMINATIONS FEDERAL RESERVE NOTES
$500, $1,000, $5,000, $10,000 and $100,000 were printed for use in transactions between banks. These were not circulated among the general public.

1929
STANDARDIZED DESIGN
To lower printing costs and standardize currency, notes were redesigned; small-size notes with standardized dimensions and common portrait locations were introduced.

1934
$100,000 GOLD CERTIFICATES
The highest denomination note ever issued, these were used only for transactions between Federal Reserve Banks and were not circulated to the public.

1957
"IN GOD WE TRUST"
$1 Silver certificates were the first notes to have the motto "In God We Trust".

1990
NEW COUNTERFEIT DETERRENT METHODS
Micro-printing and security threads are developed to deter counterfeiters.

1996-2000
REDESIGN OF PAPER MONEY
Multiple redesigned denominations introduce new portraits, watermark features and enhanced security threads. New smaller security features are introduced.

2003-2006
UPDATED SECURITY FEATURES
Improved security features and subtle background colors are introduced on the $5, $10, $20 and $50 notes. These are the first notes to use multiple colors.

2007
NEW $5 BILL WITH AN ALL DIGITAL DESIGN
This was the first bill to feature added security devices and enhanced portrait imaging.

2010
NEW $100 BILL DESIGN
The new $100 bill is released featuring advanced security features including a blue 3D security ribbon and color-shifting bell in the inkwell, improving the bill's resistance to counterfeiting.

References
http://www.ustreas.gov/education/fact-sheets/history.shtml
http://www.moneyfactory.gov/uscurrency/uscurrencyp.pdf
http://www.frbsf.org/currency/notes/2.0/uscurrencytimeline.html

BREAK THE CYCLE:
STEPS TO PROTECT YOUR FAMILY AGAINST VIOLENCE

THE CYCLE OF DOMESTIC ABUSE

Developed in the 1970s, The Cycle of Abuse by Lenore Walker explores the stages of an abusive relationship:

TENSION

Tension lays the foundation for domestic violence. The abuser - a husband, father or boyfriend - begins to exhibit passive-aggressive behaviour toward his victim - a wife, mother or girlfriend. The relationship becomes strained and tension builds as the behaviour worsens. Victims here notoriously try to change their behaviour to soften the intensity of their partner's abuse.

INCIDENT

But as it nearly always turns out - no level of obedience on the part of the victim will stop their aggressor's rage. Passive-aggressive behaviour eventually erupts in an overt or covert act of abuse - from physical abuse; hitting, kicking, shoving or throwing objects, to full-on control behaviours; such as emotional abuse, neglect or intimidation.

RECONCILIATION

After the act of abuse is over, the victim is left shaken, questioning how she landed in this situation. At this point, the abuser starts to backtrack on his actions - and is quick to repent, resolve, and reassure his bruised victim that he'll change - that this will never happen again. He tries to provide a blanket solution to the incident - buying presents or threatening self-harm if she leaves.

CALM

The calm stage - much like the calm before the storm - is thought to extend the reconciliation period as relative peace between the couple now ensues. The victim is now in the thick of a very destructive situation - blindsided to their partner's pleas that he has changed. But the calm only lasts so long as the storm begins to brew.

THE CYCLE ENDS WITH YOU

The road to end domestic violence is not impossible. Follow these practical steps to reverse the damage before it takes hold:

Tell someone about your experience
Reach out for help and don't go it alone - speak to family or friends you trust about your situation.

Safely challenge the behaviour
If you witness actual physical violence against a woman, call the police on 131 444 or 000 in the case of emergency.

Call the NSW Domestic Violence Line
For direct advice or help, you can contact a trained and qualified domestic violence advisor on 1800 656 463.

Consider the Aurora App
If you're experiencing domestic violence right now, or are worried you might be entering the cycle, download the Aurora App (free) for domestic violence risk support and services.

Lead by example and hold yourself accountable to the actions of others
Swear never to commit, excuse, or remain silent about domestic violence. Check out The White Ribbon Campaign today.

KNOW YOUR RIGHTS—SEEK LEGAL ACTION

For legal advice and family law representation, speak to a trusted solicitor today.

extracted_text_claude_sonnet__claude_son:
Title: A CUSTOMER SAVED IS A PENNY EARNED

Subtitle: What happens when brands stop focusing solely on customer acquisition, and start paying attention to customer retention.

FANTASY VS. REALITY
- 80% of businesses believe they deliver "superior" customer service
- ONLY 8% of customers believe they have experienced SUPERIOR service from those same businesses

DAMAGE DONE
- 20% of customers left a regular service provider due to poor customer service in 2012
- 55% had an issue with a transaction or purchase based on poor service experience 
- 35% will likely temper with a customer service in the past year
- 24% of those who had their temper used social media to talk about their experience with that company

ON AVERAGE
Customers tell 15 people about GOOD EXPERIENCES, but they tell 24 about their BAD EXPERIENCES
$83 BILLION: Estimated annual loss to U.S. businesses due to poor customer service
$289: The amount each customer would have spent with a business if not for the poor customer service

CAUSES OF CUSTOMER DISSATISFACTION
- DECEPTION: 78% of consumers claim they would leave a service provider if a service claim/promise was not delivered as promised
- RUDENESS: 77% if dealing with unfriendly customer service agents
- INCOMPETENCY: 73% after having to contact customer service multiple times for the same reason
- INFLEXIBILITY: 71% when they encounter inflexible policies such as inflexible returns and poor warranties
- LATENESS: 65% after being left on hold for a long time

IT DOESN'T HAVE TO BE THIS WAY
- 85% of consumers who have got a problem say they would have stayed if handled differently 
- 67% would have stayed if their issues were resolved in their FIRST CONTACT with the business
- 54% claim they might have stayed on for doing business with the brand

WHAT CUSTOMERS EXPECT
- 46% want to speak with a real person on the phone for difficult inquiries
- HUMAN
- EASY
- FAST
- SUPPORTIVE
- 73% want easy access to customer service
- 69% want access to faster customer service
- 38% are satisfied with clear information posted to company websites for simple inquiries
- 50% feel it is extremely important that customer service representatives know their client history based on previous interactions

NICE COMPANIES FINISH FIRST
- 66% of consumers are willing to spend more with a company they believe provides EXCELLENT customer service
- 44% of consumers willing to spend more with a company providing excellent customer support prefer to support smaller companies
- 9% willing to spend more with larger companies
- IT COSTS 5 TIMES AS MUCH TO ACQUIRE NEW CUSTOMERS AS IT DOES TO KEEP THOSE YOU ALREADY HAVE

Bottom tagline:
BE NICE TO THE CUSTOMERS YOU HAVE
YOU'LL WIN THE CUSTOMERS YOU WANT

[Source attribution noted at bottom: shankman|honig]

Oct

Two convicted killers who used forged documents to gain release from prison are recaptured in Florida. Joseph Jenkins and Charles Walker used documents that appeared to contain the signatures of several high-ranking officials, but which were later revealed to be fakes.

The violin played during the sinking of the Titanic is auctioned for £260,000. It had been owned by Wallace Hartley, leader of the ship's string quartet, and was among possessions found in an East Riding, UK, house in 2006.

Greek police launch an appeal to discover the identity of a blonde four-year-old girl found in a Roma camp near Farsala, central Greece. Maria was taken by police after they found her living with a family she said not appear to resemble. (Fri 25th)

Former US vice-president Dick Cheney reveals that the he had the wireless function of his implanted defibrillator disabled out of fear that terrorists would hack into it and kill him. He has suffered from heart disease for decades.

The Uruguayan government’s drug tsar Julio Calzada tells newspaper El Pais that legal marijuana will be sold in pharmacies for $1 per gram. According to Calzada, a state-controlled marijuana market would offer “a safe place to buy a quality product”, and would help reduce crime.

Six people are killed and more than 30 are injured in a suicide bombing on a bus in Volgograd in southern Russia. The head of Russia’s Investigative Committee claims that the suspects were two women who may have come from the republic of Dagestan in the North Caucasus.

Throughout the centuries, corn has been the object
of worship, prosperity, discovery and celebration.

Corn originates in
Central America, where
the Olmec and Maya
cultivated the tall grain
in numerous varieties
throughout Mesoamerica
and worshipped it in the

introduced it to other parts
of the world because of
its ability to grow in
diverse climates.

"Corn Belt," the area of
land with the highest yield
of corn, stretches from North
Dakota to Ohio. It is such
a way of life in the American
Midwest that even the
University of Nebraska's
mascot is called
"The Cornhusker."

Corn's health
benefits include a rich
source of vitamins and
nutrients, including folic

of colorectal cancer,
hemorrhoids and heart
disease.

The Corn Palace in
Mitchell, South Dakota, was
originally built to showcase
the state's rich soil and
encourage people to settle
the area. It's now a popular
tourist destination decorated
with crop art murals and
designs made from real
corn and other grains.

VARIETY SHOW

Dent Corn
When fully ripe this variety has kernels that are hard and vitreous; commonly used for animal feed and industrial products.

Flint Corn
Also called Indian corn; kernels have a hard outer coat and a glassy appearance.

Sweet Corn
A variety with a high sugar content; kernels are eaten fresh or canned.

Popcorn
A special type of corn that pops when heated, producing a fluffy, edible treat.

Flour Corn
Kernels that are soft and starchy; ground into flour for baking and cooking.

Pod Corn
A variety whose kernels are enclosed in protective husks; not commonly grown commercially.

CORNucopia

One of the most versatile grains, corn can be cooked, stewed, dried and ground into many different culinary creations and industrial products.

1
Only popcorn kernels can pop. Each kernel contains a small amount of water that is trapped inside the hard outer casing.

2
When heated to around 450°F, the moisture turns to steam, creating pressure within.

3
The casing gives way and the kernel explodes, turning the kernel inside out. Now the popcorn is ready to eat.

A-MAIZE-IN FACTS

Throughout the centuries, corn has been the object of worship, prosperity, discovery and celebration.

Corn originates in Central America, where the oldest cultivated varieties of maize were developed from their wild ancestors.

Corn was spread throughout the Western Hemisphere by Native Americans and later explorers and settlers.

The American “Corn Belt” is the area of land with the highest yield of corn, stretching from North Dakota to Ohio.

The Corn Palace in Mitchell, South Dakota, was originally built to showcase corn and celebrate the crop.

Corn’s health benefits include a rich source of vitamins and minerals, including thiamin, niacin, vitamin B6, folate, magnesium, and dietary fiber.

Corn has played a central role in many cultures and cuisines, contributing to prosperity, innovation, and celebration.

The Corn Palace, Mitchell, South Dakota, is a famous example of corn-inspired art and culture.

Sources: Betty Fussell’s The Story of Corn; Gail Gibbons’ Corn; Charles Michener’s The Life and Times of Corn; University of Minnesota Extension; http://www.organicalng.org/health-benefits-of-corn.html; http://www.popcorn.org/whats-in-popcorn

COVID-19
Coronavirus Disease 2019

BE AWARE.
COVID-19 is a new respiratory illness that was first discovered in Wuhan, China. It is transmitted from person to person.

COMMON SYMPTOMS:
- Runny nose
- Cough
- Sore throat
- High fever
- Pneumonia
- Severe respiratory disease

*Symptoms may appear 1 to 12 days following exposure to the virus

Who is most at risk of becoming seriously ill?
- People over age 60
- People with pre-existing conditions such as diabetes and heart disease

How is it transmitted?
- Through close contact with an infected person
- By an infected person coughing or sneezing
- By touching contaminated objects or surfaces and then touching your mouth, nose or eyes

Currently there is no vaccine or specific treatment. We can only treat the symptoms.
Serious cases may require oxygen and ventilatory support.

PREPARE.
- Make sure you get your information from a reliable source.
- Wash your hands regularly with soap and water or use an alcohol-based gel.
- Avoid sharing cups, plates or other personal items and disinfect all surfaces that are touched frequently.

ACT.
- Avoid close contact with people who have flu-like symptoms.
- Avoid touching your eyes, nose or mouth with unwashed hands.
- Cover your mouth with the inside of your elbow when you cough or sneeze or use a tissue and dispose of used tissue immediately and wash your hands.
- If you have traveled to areas where COVID-19 is circulating or have been in contact with someone who has it and you experience fever, cough or difficulty breathing, seek medical attention immediately. Do not self-medicate.

Note: Information may change when more is known about the disease.
February 2020.

BE AWARE. PREPARE. ACT.
www.paho.org/coronavirus

PAHO [Pan American Health Organization] [World Health Organization]

(The Infinity Stones - central wheel)

THE INFINITY STONES

Space Stone
- Also known as the Tesseract
- Powers: control of space, teleportation, portals
- Appearances: Captain America: The First Avenger (origin), The Avengers (tesseract), Thor (brief), Avengers: Infinity War (sought by Thanos)

Mind Stone
- Housed in Vision's forehead in Avengers: Age of Ultron
- Powers: control minds, consciousness, sentience
- Appearances: Loki's scepter (given by HYDRA/Chitauri), Ultron, Vision, Avengers: Infinity War

Reality Stone
- A.K.A. The Aether
- Powers: alter reality, matter manipulation
- Appearances: Thor: The Dark World, Guardians of the Galaxy? (connected to Collector), Avengers: Infinity War

Power Stone
- Appears as Power Orb
- Powers: raw energy, destroys planets, enhances strength
- Appearances: Guardians of the Galaxy (fully showcased), Avengers: Infinity War

Time Stone
- Housed in the Eye of Agamotto
- Powers: manipulate time, reverse/accelerate/loop
- Appearances: Doctor Strange, Avengers: Infinity War

Soul Stone
- Location unknown for most films; poses greatest mystery
- Powers: unknown fully, potentially controls souls and life/death
- Appearances: teased, sought by Thanos in Avengers: Infinity War

Stone Colors (center labels)
- Space (blue)
- Mind (yellow)
- Reality (red)
- Power (purple)
- Time (green)
- Soul (orange)

(Each stone sector lists cinematic clues, guardians, weaknesses and who has possessed it; small text and icons detail appearances across movies)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

UNIVERSES & HOLLYWOOD (right column)

MARVEL CINEMATIC UNIVERSE
- Run by Marvel Studios (owned by Walt Disney Company)
- Started with Iron Man in 2008
- Includes crossover films (Avengers) and solo films linked by post-credit scenes and shared characters
- MCU is a shared universe primarily focused on Earth's heroes and cosmic threats

DC EXTENDED UNIVERSE
- Warner Bros' cinematic universe for DC Comics characters
- Key films include Man of Steel, Batman v Superman, Suicide Squad, Wonder Woman, Justice League
- Darker, standalone tone compared to many MCU films

20th CENTURY FOX
- Studio owning film rights to X-Men, Fantastic Four characters (until acquisition)
- Separate shared universe from MCU in which mutants and FF exist

SONY PICTURES
- Owns cinema rights to Spider-Man and associated characters
- Collaborates with Marvel Studios on Spider-Man: Homecoming and future appearances

(Additional note)
- Agents, contracts and studio rights determine which characters can appear where.
- Cross-studio team-ups are rare but possible (e.g., Sony-Marvel agreement on Spider-Man)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

TIMELINE ANNOTATIONS (around main circle, selected film notes)

IRON MAN (2008) – Tony Stark builds Iron Man suit, introduced to MCU
THE INCREDIBLE HULK (2008) – Banner's struggle with the Hulk; ties to Thunderbolt Ross; post-credits scene hints at linking with Iron Man
IRON MAN 2 (2010) – Tony faces Justin Hammer, War Machine (James Rhodes) introduced; Natasha Romanoff (Black Widow) appears
THOR (2011) – Thor banished to Earth, Loki's mischief begins; introduces Asgard and the cosmic scale
CAPTAIN AMERICA: THE FIRST AVENGER (2011) – WWII, the Tesseract origin
THE AVENGERS (2012) – Earth-first big team-up; Chitauri invasion, Tesseract central
IRON MAN 3 (2013) – Tony deals with PTSD after New York; Extremis tech
THOR: THE DARK WORLD (2013) – Introduces Reality Stone (the Aether)
CAPTAIN AMERICA: THE WINTER SOLDIER (2014) – Hydra revealed inside S.H.I.E.L.D.; Bucky's past
GUARDIANS OF THE GALAXY (2014) – Introduces cosmic side, Power Stone, Thanos family ties
AVENGERS: AGE OF ULTRON (2015) – Ultron created, Vision and Mind Stone revealed
ANT-MAN (2015) – Scott Lang becomes Ant-Man; Pym tech, Quantum Realm teases
CAPTAIN AMERICA: CIVIL WAR (2016) – Avengers split; Spider-Man and Black Panther introduced (to film canon)
DOCTOR STRANGE (2016) – Time Stone and Sorcerer mystic arts established
GUARDIANS OF THE GALAXY VOL. 2 (2017) – Ego, family revelations, cosmic stakes
SPIDER-MAN: HOMECOMING (2017) – Spider-Man integrated into MCU
THOR: RAGNAROK (2017) – Asgard destroyed; Hulk and Thor reunite; introduces Valkyrie, Grandmaster
BLACK PANTHER (2018) – Wakanda introduced; Vibranium, political stakes
AVENGERS: INFINITY WAR (2018) – Convergence of heroes; Thanos' quest for all 6 Infinity Stones; global and cosmic stakes

(Various small bubbles show character appearances in each film and star ratings / critic notations in tiny text)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

BOTTOM LEFT – THE "false" gauntlet

The "false" gauntlet
- There was much debate about whether Thanos' golden glove shown in early photos was the actual Infinity Gauntlet or a prop.
- Early leaks of the "gauntlet" turned out to be a physical prop used on set but not the real Cosmic Gauntlet with fully functioning stones.
- What appears in the trailers is not the final design; the movie's actual gauntlet may be different in color and detail.
- The gauntlet in the movie houses all SIX INFINITY STONES and allows the user to harness their combined powers. When Thanos uses it, it can erase half of all life in the universe with a snap.

(Small caption near glove image)
- As seen in the film: Thanos will use the gauntlet to channel the energy of the stones.
- NOTE: Visual effects teams combine on-set props with CGI to create the final look.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

BOTTOM CENTER – ANT-MAN & THE YELLOW ARROW TIMELINE

ANT-MAN (2015) – Scott Lang introduced; Pym tech and shrinking; connects to later events
ANT-MAN AND THE WASP (2018) – Events concurrent to Infinity War; explores Quantum Realm and Hope van Dyne

THE INFINITY STONES (central wheel)

The Infinity Stones

Space Stone (BLUE)
- Located in Tesseract
- Powers: Teleportation, creates wormholes, moves matter across space
- MCU appearances: Captain America: The First Avenger; The Avengers; Thor: The Dark World?; etc

Mind Stone (YELLOW)
- Located in Loki's scepter; later in Vision's forehead
- Powers: Mind control, intelligence enhancement, sentience
- MCU appearances: The Avengers; Avengers: Age of Ultron; Captain America: Civil War; etc

Reality Stone (RED) also known as the Aether
- Fluid-like red matter
- Powers: Alters reality, physics; can create illusions and rewrite the fabric of reality
- MCU appearances: Thor: The Dark World; Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2? (Thanos collects it)

Power Stone (PURPLE)
- Located in the Orb (Xandar)
- Powers: Great destructive power; amplifies other stones; can destroy planets
- MCU appearances: Guardians of the Galaxy

Time Stone (GREEN)
- Located in the Eye of Agamotto
- Powers: Manipulation of time — rewind, fast-forward, create time loops; immortality implications
- MCU appearances: Doctor Strange; Avengers: Infinity War

Soul Stone (ORANGE)
- Location: Vormir (sacrifice required)
- Powers: Controls souls, essence of life and death
- MCU appearances: Unknown before Infinity War; sacrificed for in Infinity War

CENTER TEXT AROUND WHEEL
- Each stone has short descriptors and known appearances
(e.g., Space — Tesseract; Mind — Scepter/Vision; Reality — Aether; Power — Orb; Time — Eye of Agamotto; Soul — Vormir)

LEGENDS / ICONS AROUND WHEEL
- Tiny icons of characters aligned to stones and movies
- Small ratings and critical reception bubbles near some films (star ratings visible)

UNIVERSES & HOLLYWOOD (Right column)

Universes & Hollywood

Marvel Cinematic Universe
- The idea of a shared universe was initially introduced in the 1960s comics. In the MCU, the films are explicitly linked and characters cross over from film to film, with an overall throughline driven by the Infinity Stones and Thanos' quest to collect them all.
- Marvel Studios produced the films above as part of Phases One, Two and Three.
- The MCU is known for mid-credits and post-credits scenes that set up future movies.

DC Extended Universe
- DC Entertainment and Warner Bros. Pictures created the DC Extended Universe (DCEU). The DCEU attempts to create a similar shared universe to the MCU with characters like Superman, Batman, Wonder Woman, Aquaman and the Flash appearing across titles, although the tone and approach differ.

20th CENTURY FOX
- Although they announced plans to bring the X-Men into a shared universe, these plans were carried out in their own continuity, separate from DC and the MCU.

SONY PICTURES
- Sony controls the film rights to Spider-Man and some associated characters. A deal with Marvel allows Spider-Man to appear in the MCU (e.g., Captain America: Civil War; Spider-Man: Homecoming) while Sony still produces standalone Spider-Man films.

THE "FALSE" GAUNTLET (left bottom block near Infinity Gauntlet image)

The "false" gauntlet

- In Avengers: Age of Ultron, Tony Stark and Bruce Banner create a prototype gauntlet for Thor to wield that looks similar to the Infinity Gauntlet but is not the true gauntlet Thanos uses.
- This gauntlet was created as a prop and cosmetic measure for battle, not as the actual artifact capable of channeling all six Infinity Stones.
- The real Infinity Gauntlet that Thanos obtains is sturdier, properly forged to harness the Stones' powers, and is shown in Avengers: Infinity War with the Stones embedded.

An educated population is necessary for a country to remain competitive in the global marketplace, but when personal and social decisions driving demand don’t align with that goal.

There’s no doubt an education is necessary, yet when it comes to grad school, for some the costs outweigh the pros.

WHO’S FIGHTING THE WAR ON GRAD SCHOOL? // POLITICAL REFORMS

Tax Reform Act of 1986
Payments for teaching and research assistantships that previously qualified as scholarships are now considered taxable income for students.

Technical and Miscellaneous Revenue Act of 1988
Technical and miscellaneous retirement benefits are taxed.

College Cost Reduction and Access Act of 2007
Decrease in interest rate on subsidized Stafford loans for undergraduate students.
No change in interest rates for grad students.

Budget Control Act of 2011
Grad students no longer eligible for subsidized Stafford loans.
Funding cuts to discretionary spending and research programs.

Bipartisan Student Loan Certainty Act of 2013
Grad students saw a hike in interest rates.
2.6% on Federal Stafford Loans
2.5% on Federal GRAD PLUS Loans

2016 Tax Reform
Originally proposed taxing the income of graduate teaching and research assistantships.
Proposed changes not enacted into law.

ARE THE CRITICS RIGHT?
Reasons not to pursue a graduate education

Strained relationship with significant other

Graduate students must give up:
Travel
Work experience
Vacations

Ultimate:
Will students regret time spent on grad school?

Reasons to pursue a graduate education

1 Higher salary

3 Make professional connections

Making Grad School Work

Pinterest
Key differentiating trait:
More of a niche platform, despite very large usage. Great for businesses that involve planning, design, creation, making. Heavily female audience.
Best for: Link clicks
Resource and expertise level needed: High
Good for these business types:

- Interior design

Twitter
Key differentiating trait:
Fast-moving, real-time, news-driven platform. Strong customer service element, be prepared to engage and respond to customers here.
Best for: Link clicks, engagement, and foot traffic
Resource and expertise level needed: Low
Good for these business types:
- Retail
- Nonprofits
- Newspapers & media outlets

LinkedIn
Key differentiating trait:
Unlike almost every other platform, which is designed for the user to focus on their personal interests, this is a profession-driven platform.
Best for: Link clicks, event registrations
Resource and expertise level needed: Medium
Good for these business types:
- Luxury goods & services
- Software providers
- B2B Companies

Key differentiating trait:

Best for: Foot traffic, word-of-mouth awareness, engagement
Resource and expertise level needed: Medium to high
Good for these business types:
- Bakeries & Coffee shops

Key differentiating trait:

That being said, there's still no better place to find and engage customers, donors, or others.
Best for: Link clicks, word-of-mouth awareness, event registrations
Resource and expertise level needed: Low

- Local Brick & Mortar Stores

- B2B Companies

A-MAIZE-ING FACTS

Throughout the centuries, corn has been the object
of worship, prosperity, discovery and celebration.

Corn originates in
Central America, where
the Olmec and Maya
cultivated the tall grain
in numerous varieties
throughout Mesoamerica
and worshipped it in the
form of their Tonantzin
Maize God. The Aztecs
also had their own maize
deities, Centeotl and
Chicomecoatl.

Corn was spread
throughout the
Western Hemisphere by
various Native Americans.
Early European explorers
were enlightened about
the New World grain in the
15th and 16th centuries, and later
introduced it to other parts
of the world because of
its ability to grow in
diverse climates.

The American
"Corn Belt," the area of
land with the highest yield
of corn, stretches from North
Dakota to Ohio. It is such
a way of life in the American
Midwest that even the
University of Nebraska's
mascot is called
"The Cornhusker."

Corn's health
benefits include a rich
source of vitamins and
nutrients, including folic
acid, niacin and vitamin C.
High in fiber, it is also
believed to reduce the risk
of colorectal cancer,
hemorrhoids and heart
disease.

The Corn Palace in
Mitchell, South Dakota, was
originally built to showcase
the state's rich soil and
encourage people to settle
the area. It's now a popular
tourist destination decorated
with crop art murals and
designs made from real
corn and other grains.

A-MAIZE-NG FACTS

Throughout the centuries, corn has been the object of worship, prosperity, discovery and celebration.

Corn originates in Central America, where the Olmec and Maya cultivated the tall grain that was later spread by traders and explorers.

The American “Corn Belt” is the area of the United States where corn production is highest, including parts of the Midwest.

The Corn Palace in Mitchell, South Dakota, is a famous monument to corn and its cultural importance.

Love at First Sight
Within milliseconds, your medial prefrontal cortex tells you they're attractive.

Hormones and neurochemicals from your brain flood your body.

1. SEXUAL DESIRE
- Possible without attachment or infatuation
- Can rise and fall during long-term relationships
- Testosterone increases in both men and women
- Estrogen increases in women

2. LIMERENCE

COLOR PROFILES & PRINTING EXPLAINED
"What's the Difference?"
How do RGB and PANTONE industry standards for color profiles compare and what are their uses? Find out now!

CMYK
- CMYK is the standard color profile for printing. Typically assigned before sending to your Pantone swatch.
- CMYK USE: CMYK is used for printed materials. CMYK colors do not always exactly match what you see on your screen (like a Pantone swatch).

RGB
- RGB refers to the three primary colors: RED, GREEN and BLUE
- RGB USE: RGB is the color profile used for digital screens and devices. Your screen uses light (rather than ink) to produce the bright colors you see.

PANTONE
- The Pantone Matching System (PMS) is used to standardize colors.
- PMS USE: Pantone Matching System can be used to accurately match colors for branding across all mediums.

What About Printing?

SPOT COLOR (OFFSET)
- Spot color printing describes the process where specialized ink is mixed according to a formula to achieve a specific color, such as a Pantone swatch.
- BEST FOR: Spot color printing would be typically used for jobs which require only one or two colors, such as logo/artwork spot color printing or text.

FOUR COLOR OFFSET
- 4 Color process printing refers to the CMYK printing process. The colors Cyan, Magenta, Yellow and Black will be mixed together to produce a full color spectrum.
- BEST FOR: Four color process is the most common form of printing for 3 colors or more. With up to 50,000+ pieces, offset printing may be the most cost-effective.

DIGITAL PRINTING
- Digital printing uses either toner or ink-jet technology. Digital printing right off your CMYK file will show ink variations in proofs vs final prints.
- BEST FOR: Although it has been considered in the past to be the up-charge vs offset printing, technology continues to improve making this an excellent option for short runs or fast-time printer.

COLOR

Profiles & Printing Explained

What's the Difference?
CMYK, RGB, and Pantone are industry standards for color profiles. How do they compare and what are their uses? Find out now!

CMYK

CMYK is the standard color profile for printing. Typically, your printer uses CYAN, MAGENTA, YELLOW, and BLACK inks to print colored images. When all colors are mixed together, an rich black is produced.

RGB

RGB refers to the three primary colors, RED, GREEN and BLUE. RGB colors use light to make their colors bright. If you mix them all together you would get pure white.

PANTONE

The Pantone Matching System (PMS) is used to correctly match materials with their corresponding RGB or CMYK colors. This insures that a color looks the same each time it is produced when matched to a Pantone swatch.

CMYK USE

CMYK is used for printed materials. CMYK colors do not appear as bright as RGB (they are missing the added bonus of a light source). Brochures, business cards, posters, etc. may be printed in CMYK.

RGB USE

RGB is the color profile used for digital screens and devices. Your screen mixes red, green, and blue light to produce the bright colors you see on your display. Websites, mobile apps, video, etc. are in RGB.

PMS USE

Pantone Matching System can be used to accurately match colors for anything from fabrics and paints to t-shirts and printed materials.

Knowing the differences between the color profiles can help you to understand how different printing processes work and how those processes will affect your completed project.

Spot color printing describes the process where specialized ink or specially blended ink is created to make a perfect match that corresponds to your desired color, such as a Pantone swatch.

If there is just one color to be printed, there will be a single plate, and a single run of the press. If there are two colors, there will be two plates and two runs, and so on. The colors are layered onto the paper one by one.

BEST FOR:
Spot color printing would be typically used for jobs which require no full color imagery, such as for business cards and other stationery, or in monotone (or duotone etc) literature such as black and white newspaper print.

If you want something printed that has a unique color, like a metallic or fluorescent, spot color printing will be needed.

FOUR COLOR (OFFSET)

C M Y K =

4 Color process printing refers to the CMYK printing process. This means your artwork will be printed using only Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, and Black ink colors.

Just like spot color printing, each color is run through one at a time. The layering of each ink color produces a final product with a full color spectrum.

BEST FOR:
Four color process is the most common form of printing and is best for large batches, like 500 postcards. It produces clear, sharp prints.

* In some cases it may be possible to do a six-color or eight-color print. This type of process is more expensive, but will produce a wider range of colors.

DIGITAL PRINTING

Digital printing uses either toner or ink-jet technology. Digital printing might still use CMYK, but all colors are applied at once instead of with the "offset" roller method.

Although it has been considered in the past to not be as "crisp" as offset printing, technologies continue to improve and digital printing can produce some great results.

BEST FOR:
Digital printing is recommended for small batches, like 20 invitations or just one poster.

* Because digital printing does not require the creation of printing plates, turn-around time is often much faster.

Thanks to social media, individuals and organizations have been empowered to advance social change in education like never before. Check out how some of the pros are doing it, and brush up on a few ways you can help, too.

Stats on Supporting Social Change

73% of U.S. adults say being involved in social change is personally important to them.

Likelihood to engage in positive social change when economic conditions are bad:
60% Women
53% Men
69% Join Conversation
53% Start Conversation

U.S. adults who say they have engaged in positive social change by donating money, goods or services.

People are more likely to join digital social change conversations than to start them.

I want to help people who are less fortunate than I am
71%
61%
Ages 18-34
Ages 41+

4 out of 5 adults believe “I can make the world a better place by my actions.”

Making Social Change

With their ties to social media, these organizations are making it a snap for millions of people to help their causes.

Kickstarter
Kickstarter’s all-or-nothing funding method helps projects reach their funding goals. This allows people to test how much support there is for a project. Visit: http://www.kickstarter.com/

DonorsChoose.org
Teachers post their classroom needs, everything from pencils to microscopes; people can donate online to whichever need they like. Once the project reaches its funding goal the materials are delivered to the school. Photos of the project and progress are sent to donors. Visit: http://www.donorschoose.org/

Pencils of Promise
This non-profit builds schools and educates children in developing countries. Instead of spending big budgets on advertising, PoP developed one of the largest non-profit campaigns online. Folks can donate on the site or bid on artwork through their Made with Pencils campaign. Visit: http://www.pencilsofpromise.org/

CrowdRise
Folks can start their own fundraising campaign on the site or join someone else’s project team and help them. Although not dedicated to educational efforts, CrowdRise lists thousands of school related projects, charities and more. Visit: http://www.crowdrise.com/give

Investigating Data Scientists
Understanding their Skills, Roles and Team Makeup

A Study of 600+ Data Scientists*
Respondents provided self-assessment ratings about proficiency in 5 skill areas:
1. Business
2. Technology
3. Programming
4. Math
5. Statistics

Respondents provided information about their team makeup, job role, industry, satisfaction with work outcome, gender and more.

Most respondents were from B2B companies (80%), North America (64%). About 46% work for companies with less than 100 employees

*Results reflect data collected from AnalyticsWeek and Business of... Respondents completed surveys using AnalyticsWeek newsletter, blog posts, AnalyticsWeek and social media. (small print)

Top 5 Industries Employing Data Scientists
1. Information Technology (26%)
2. Education / Science (14%)
3. Consulting (13%)
4. Financial Services (11%)
5. Healthcare & Medical (9%)

Data Scientists are Highly Educated
Most data scientists hold graduate degrees (~67%)
Nearly half of data scientists hold a Master's degree
There was no difference between males and females in their educational attainment

Data Science Skills

Top 10 Data Science Skills
1. Communication
2. Structured Data
3. Data Mining and Visualization Tools
4. Math / Scientific Method
5. Statistics
6. Programming
7. Data Management
8. Machine Learning / Predictive Modeling
9. Product Design and Development
10. Business Development

Skill Areas of Data Science
Subject Matter
- Industry/domain expertise
- Business process knowledge
- Product management, marketing, operations knowledge
Programming & Technology
- Data collection, data cleaning, ETL, data engineering
- Databases, distributed computing, cloud computing, APIs
- Tools: R, Python, Java, Scala, SQL, Hadoop, Spark
Statistics
- Statistical modeling and inference
- Regression, classification, clustering, time-series
- Experimental design, A/B testing, causal inference

(Notes: radar/scatter/visualizations illustrating skill distributions; small footnote about survey methodology and definitions)

Data Science Roles

Four Types of Data Scientists
"Researcher" is the most popular data science job role

Business Managers include leaders, business persons and entrepreneurs
Developers include developers and engineers
Creatives includes Jack of all trades, artists and hackers
Researchers include scientists and statisticians

Data Scientists are not Created Equal
- Business Managers understand business (i.e., subject matter) but not tech or stats
- Developers understand technology and programming but not business or stats
- Creatives possess a basic knowledge in the three skill areas
- Researchers are strong in statistics and math but weak in business and programming

(visual: circular/radar chart showing strengths and weaknesses by role)

Data Science is a Team Sport

Diverse Data Science Teams Perform Better
Researchers and Developers report better outcomes of analytics projects when they work with Business experts

Business Managers report better outcomes of analytics projects when they work with Statistics experts

Bigger Teams, Bigger Outcomes
Data scientists who work with 4 or more people on their analytics projects report better outcomes compared to their counterparts who work with fewer people.

Getting Insights from your Data

The Scientific Method
1. Formulate a question or problem statement.
2. Generate a hypothesis that is testable.
3. Gather / Generate data through experimentation or observation.
4. Analyze data / Draw conclusions (e.g., test hypotheses).
5. Communicate results / Take action (e.g., change processes) based on the conclusions. Refine hypotheses for further testing.

The scientific method is a way of investigating phenomena, acquiring new knowledge, or correcting and integrating previous knowledge.

(visual: cycle: Hypothesis -> Data -> Analysis -> Results/Conclusion)

Three Primary Skills of Data Scientists
(visual Venn diagram: Subject Matter, Statistics, Technology)
1. Subject Matter expertise helps you know what questions you need to ask.
2. Statistics expertise helps you apply the right analytics to answer your questions.
3. Technology expertise helps you access the data and process it quickly.

How to Apply Data Science Skills to the Scientific Method
(The scientific method helps data scientists organize their collective efforts to extract insight and value from data.)

1. Formulate a question or problem statement
   - Subject Matter
2. Generate a hypothesis / deductive guess
   - Subject Matter + Statistics
3. Gather / Generate data
   - Technology
4. Analyze / test hypotheses / draw conclusions
   - Statistics + Technology
5. Communicate results / Take action
   - Subject Matter + Communication

Learn More about the Practice of Data Science
For more detailed information about the results contained in this infographic, check out these articles:
- Investigating Data Scientists: their Skills and Team Makeup http://bit.ly/...
- Getting Insights Using Data Science Skills and the Scientific Method http://bit.ly/...
- Data Science Roles and the Practice of Data Science http://bit.ly/...
- Top 10 Skills in Data Science http://bit.ly/...
- Team Science Trends Report White Paper Outcomes http://bit.ly/...

(footer logos and small links)
www.businessof...com | labs@businessof...com | www.analyticsweek.com | AnalyticsWeek

(brand logos: Business of, AnalyticsWeek)

Most data scientists hold graduate degrees (~67%)

Top 10 Data Science Skills
1. Communication
2. Structured Data
3. Data Mining and Visualization Tools
4. Science / Scientific Method
5. Statistics

7. Data Management
8. Data Analysis and Visualization
9. Software Development / Modeling
10. Business Development

Skill Areas of Data Science
1. Subject Matter Expertise
   - Domain knowledge, industry understanding, and the ability to interpret data in a business context
2. Statistics
   - Experimental design, hypothesis testing, sampling, modeling
3. Technology
   - Tools, platforms, programming, data engineering, and scalable solutions

Data Science Roles
Four Types of Data Scientists
- Researchers
  Includes scientists and statisticians (focus on theory, models, and methodology)
- Developers
  Includes programmers and data engineers (focus on building systems and pipelines)
- Creatives
  Includes analysts and communicators who turn data into actionable insights for decision-makers
- Data Scientists are not Created Equal
  Differences in background, expertise, and focus across roles

Data Science is a Team Sport
Diverse Data Science Teams Perform Better

- Cross-functional collaboration leads to stronger outcomes on analytics projects

Data Scientists with Bigger Teams Deliver Bigger Outcomes
Bigger Teams, Bigger Outcomes
- Data scientists who work with 4 or more people on analytics projects report better outcomes than those with fewer teammates

Getting Insights from your Data
The Scientific Method
- Formulate a question or problem
- Generate a hypothesis
- Gather data and prepare data
- Analyze and draw conclusions
- Communicate results and make decisions

Three Primary Skills of Data Scientists
1. Statistics
2. Technology
3. Subject Matter

How to Apply Data Science Skills to the Scientific Method
- Map each data science skill to steps in the scientific method
- Use statistics to test hypotheses
- Apply technology to gather, clean, and analyze data
- Leverage subject matter expertise to interpret results and guide decisions

TAPPING THE MARKET
Expanding Access to Sanitation for the Poor Through the Domestic Private Sector

2.5 BILLION People across the developing world use shared or unimproved sanitation facilities

A CLOSER LOOK: $2.6 BILLION Total estimated market across Bangladesh, Indonesia, Peru, and Tanzania (based on one time sales of improved sanitation facilities to those without access)

Countries analyzed:
BANGLADESH, INDONESIA, PERU, TANZANIA

What is the size of the population without access to improved sanitation?
- Bangladesh: 16 MILLION
- Indonesia: 100 MILLION
- Peru: 8.7 MILLION
- Tanzania: 40.5 MILLION

What is the estimated annual economic cost of inadequate sanitation?
- Bangladesh: $4.2 BILLION
- Indonesia: $6.3 BILLION
- Peru: $759 MILLION
- Tanzania: $206 MILLION

What is the average cost of latrine/toilet pumping as a percentage of average monthly household income?
- Bangladesh: 39%
- Indonesia: 44%
- Peru: 89%
- Tanzania: 48%

What percent of enterprises identify the poor as target customers?
- Bangladesh: 62%
- Indonesia: 66%
- Peru: N/A
- Tanzania: 48%

WHAT ARE THE FACTORS DRIVING DEMAND?
[For each country]:
Bangladesh: GDP Growth, Poverty, Sanitation Marketing, Household Subsidies
Indonesia: GDP Growth, Poverty Decline, Sanitation Marketing, Policy Supported Public-Private Partnerships
Peru: GDP Growth, Poverty Decline, Private Sector Interest in Sanitation Programs, Dissatisfaction with Current Solutions
Tanzania: GDP Growth, Poverty Decline, Sanitation Marketing, Unmet Sanitation Needs

KEY CHALLENGES:
- Unmet Customer Needs
- Weak Supply Chain
- Difficult Investment Climate

RECOMMENDATIONS:
1. Stimulate Demand
- Show consumers that improved sanitation is a smart economic decision
- Design sanitation solutions that match customer needs and preferences
- Offer flexible financing options

2. Strengthen the Sanitation Supply Chain
- Build business capacity and professionalism
- Encourage formation of strategic partnerships to develop proper distribution channels to reach remote customers

3. Improve the Investment Climate
- Communicate clear policies that encourage private sector entry
- Facilitate private sector provision by clearly defining the role of the private sector in government strategy and policy

For more information, visit www.wsp.org/dpsp

[Logos]: IFC International Finance Corporation, wsp, THE WORLD BANK

MARKET DRIVERS

WHAT ARE CONSTRAINTS TO INVESTMENT?

UNMET CUSTOMER NEEDS
"Why should I pay for a toilet or latrine?"
"Why should I settle if I can't afford the dream?"

The poor are challenging customers who would rather make do with their current sanitation situation if they cannot find a desirable, affordable product in their local market.

WEAK SUPPLY CHAIN
"How do I reach poor households?"
"Why should I prioritize sanitation in my portfolio?"

The sanitation market is highly fragmented and uncoordinated, making it difficult to develop and deliver products that consumers are willing and able to buy. Enterprises with the scale to tackle these industry challenges do not currently see sanitation or the poor as priority markets; businesses that do focus on sanitation have limited reach and product differentiation.

DIFFICULT INVESTMENT CLIMATE
"How can we improve the ease of doing business in the sanitation sector?"
"Why should we invest in this even riskier business?"

Current sanitation policies are not focused on lifting barriers to market entry. Despite the existence of government sanitation promotion programs, sanitation enterprises do not have enough information about viable markets and lack sufficient access to finance.

KEY CHALLENGES

RECOMMENDATIONS

1
Stimulate Demand
• Show consumers that good sanitation is a smart economic decision
• Design sanitation solutions that meet poor customers' needs and preferences
• Offer flexible financing options

2
Strengthen the Sanitation Supply Chain
• Build business capacity and market intelligence
• Encourage formation of strategic partnerships to develop stronger distribution channels to reach remote customers

3
Improve the Investment Climate
• Communicate clear policies that promote improved sanitation
• Facilitate private sector provision by clearly defining the role of the private sector in government strategy and policy

For more information, visit www.wsp.org/dpsp

* All dollar amounts provided are in US dollars
* Some figures have been rounded; please see the full publication for more details

HDL ("GOOD") CHOLESTEROL

THYROID

Effects:
- Cause weight gain and

- Increase cholesterol levels due to inhibition of the body's ability to metabolize energy

Because we spend less time in the sun than our ancestors, more than 75% of US adults have insufficient levels of Vitamin D. This has been associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, cancer, and infection.

Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) are remote-controlled aircraft that can carry cameras, sensors and weapons over enemy territory. The decade since 9/11 has seen these remote aircraft increase in prominence from speculative prototypes to America's primary counterterrorism weapon.

The largest drone...
With a range of 10,000 miles (16,000 kilometers), the Northrop Grumman RQ-4 Global Hawk cruises at high altitude, loitering over an area for up to 30 hours. The drone produces high-quality surveillance images using its suite of sensors.

Allison Rolls-Royce turbofan engine has 7,050 lb (3,200 kg) thrust

Satellite communications antenna

Sensor suite includes synthetic aperture radar, electro-optical sensors and infrared sensors

Wings composed of high-strength, lightweight graphite composite material

... and the smallest

At the other end of the spectrum, the RQ-11 Raven is so tiny, a soldier can heave it into the air one- handed. Once airborne, the Raven has a range of 7.45 miles (12 km). Future drones could be the size of a hummingbird or even an insect.

On the Battlefield: Use of Drones in Combat

1 Oct. 7, 2001: The first armed Predator drones begin flying missions over Afghanistan less than a month after 9/11. By March 2002, Drones would begin providing close air support for troops on the ground.

2 Nov. 3, 2002: A CIA-operated Predator drone launched a Hellfire missile at a car carrying five al-Qaida members. It was the first drone assassination in what would become a decadelong campaign.

3 Dec. 23, 2002: A Predator drone fires a sidewinder missile at an Iraqi fighter plane in the No Fly Zone. This is the first instance of air-to-air combat with a drone. During the surge in 2007, drone surveillance would significantly reduce the number of roadside bomb attacks.

4 May 7, 2004: The CIA begins a covert drone bombing campaign in Pakistan's tribal regions. Over the next four years, President George Bush would order 42 more drone strikes in the region. From 2008 to 2011, President Barack Obama has ordered at least 180 strikes. Some estimates put the death toll from the campaign at close to 2,000.

5 Late 2004: The U.S. Border Patrol begins testing UAVs for patrolling the U.S./Mexico border. In 2009, the U.S. begins flying drones inside of Mexican airspace in support of Mexican government antidrug cartel operations.

6 Late 2009: U.S. begins drone flights off of Somalia to monitor and deter pirates. In June 2011, the U.S. begins basing drones at the Mogadishu airport and launching drone missile strikes within Somalia against al-Shabab militants linked to al-Qaida.

7 April 21, 2011: President Obama approves the use of drones in support of the Libyan rebels. The drones destroy Qaddafi air defenses in advance of the NATO bombing campaign, and provide targeting and support for NATO allies during the war. Operations in Libya also feature the first use of drone helicopters in combat.

8 Jan. 25, 2011: A Texas Department of Public Safety SWAT team deploys a small drone during a raid on a drug stash house. This marks the first use of UAVs for domestic surveillance.

Title: The impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on Canadian families and children

Subtitle: We asked Canadians what it is like to be a parent during the COVID-19 pandemic. Here are some of the participants' responses:

Screen time, reading and physical activity were the most popular daily activities
Daily or almost every day:
- Screen time: 89%
- Reading books or stories: 62%
- Physical activities: 61%
- Games: 23%
- Music, drama or visual arts: 21%
- Developing other skills: 18%

Parents' top concerns were children's opportunities to socialize with friends and balancing child care, schooling and work

Percentage of participants who were very or extremely concerned about their children's:
- Opportunities to socialize with friends: 71%
- Amount of screen time: 64%
- Loneliness or isolation: 54%
- General mental health: 48%
- School year and academic success: 40%

Percentage of participants who were very or extremely concerned for their families in terms of:
- Balancing child care, schooling and work: 74%
- Managing their children's or their children's stress levels, anxiety and emotions: 61%
- Having less patience, raising their voice, or scolding or yelling at their children: 46%
- Staying connected with family or friends: 43%
- Getting along and supporting each other: 37%
- Feeling lonely in their own home: 30%

Parents of young school-aged children were the most likely to be very or extremely concerned about balancing child care, schooling and work:
- Preschool-aged children only: 70%
- School-aged children aged 4 to 11: 80%
- School-aged children aged 12 to 14: 55%
- Children of various ages: 78%

Source: Statistics Canada, Impacts of COVID-19 on Canadians: Parenting During the Pandemic, June 2020.

WOMEN AND CRIMINAL VICTIMIZATION

Violence against women is an important issue that impacts individuals, families and society as a whole. It also acts as a barrier to gender equality in Canada and around the world.

1. 2014 | OVER 1.2 MILLION VIOLENT VICTIMIZATION INCIDENTS
self-reported* by women

Violent victimizations include sexual assault, robbery and physical assault.
[Icon shows this represents 56% of all violent incidents]

2. 2015 | 88.2% OF SEXUAL ASSAULT VICTIMS ARE FEMALE

3. RISK FACTORS
Violence can affect anyone, but some factors can put individuals at higher risk of being victimized.

4. YOUNG WOMEN
aged 15 to 24
Violent victimization was the highest among young women in this age category, with 215 incidents per 1,000 population.

RATES OF VICTIMIZATION DECLINE WITH AGE
*per 1,000 population
- aged 25 to 34: 128 incidents*
- aged 35 to 44: 68 incidents*
- aged 45 to 54: 68 incidents*
- aged 55 to 64: 42 incidents*
- aged 64 and older: 14 incidents*

5. INDIGENOUS WOMEN
experienced violence at a rate 2.7 times higher than non-Indigenous women in 2014.

1980: 9% of all female homicide victims were Indigenous
2015: 24% of all female homicide victims were Indigenous

6. LESBIAN AND BISEXUAL WOMEN
experienced violence at more than twice the rate reported by gay and bisexual men in 2014.

Gay and bisexual men: 121 incidents*
Lesbian and bisexual women: 281 incidents*
*per 1,000 population

*The two primary sources of data are police-reported crime data, and self-reported victimization. Self-reported incidents may not have come to the attention of the police and were collected through the General Social Survey on Canadians' safety.

These highlights were taken from the "Women in the Criminal Justice System" chapter of the 7th edition of Women in Canada: A Gender-based Statistical Report.

extracted_text_claude_sonnet__claude_son:
THE LIFESPAN OF STORAGE MEDIA

For as long we have been inventing ways to capture and present data, we have been developing media on which to store it. With each new innovation, we've seen greater convenience, increased storage capacity, and longer lifespans. But at some point, even with the utmost care, media fails... leaving us with a useless piece of plastic, tape, or metal.

The Cloud, with its extensive network of server farms, leverages redundant storage media, ensuring that in the event of hardware failure, the data lives on, stored safely in another part of the cloud. By way of comparison, we've assembled some lifespan statistics for storage media throughout the digital ages.

Icons: FAST | REDUNDANT | RELIABLE | ACCESSIBLE ANYWHERE | FOREVER

Years of Use (Timeline from 0-100+ years):

COMPUTER MEDIA:
- ZIP DISK (1994): 2-10 years
- 5" FLOPPY (1976): 2-30 years
- 3.5" FLOPPY DISK (1982): 2-15 years
- CD-RW (1997): 3-100 years
- DIGITAL TAPE (1987): 10-30 years
- USB FLASH DRIVE (2000): 10-75 years
- DVD-R (1999): 30-100 years
- HARD DRIVE (1956): 34-100 years
- SOLID STATE HARD DRIVE (1999): 51-100+ years

AUDIO MEDIA:
- COMPACT DISC (1982): 3-100 years
- REEL AUDIO TAPE (1935): 10-20 years
- CASSETTE TAPE (1963): 10-20 years
- EIGHT TRACK (1964): 10-30 years
- MINI DISC (1992): 15-50 years
- VINYL RECORD (1881/1931): 50-100 years

VIDEO MEDIA:
- VHS TAPE (1971): 5-15 years
- DV TAPE (1995): 10-20 years
- DVD (1995): 30-100 years
- SUPER 8 FILM (1965): 70-100 years

PHOTO MEDIA:
- UNDEVELOPED FILM (1889): 3-6 years
- DIGITAL PHOTOS (1990s): 30-50 years
- PHOTO SLIDES (1961): 50-70 years
- DEVELOPED PHOTOS (1800s): 50-70 years
- MEMORY CARD (1999): 115 years

Notable Annotations:
- CLICK OF DEATH: When the disk makes a clicking sound suddenly stick. That signified that the disk can no longer usable and all data is lost.
- LOST IN SPACE: USB drives are often lost long before they ever fail
- HEAD CRASH: When the inside your drives hits the platter or cylinder and crashes the drive
- BE KIND REWIND: VHS tapes would often tear or break due to mechanical stress
- VIDEO "HITS": Degrading digital tapes will drop frames and pause during playback
- COLOR FADES: B&W Photos will last longer than color ones. The dyes in colors are less stable
- GRANDMASTER FLASH: Metal tends to rust and degrade over time

ADDITIONAL WAYS MEDIA CAN FAIL:
- COMPATIBILITY ISSUES
- OXIDATION/CHEMICAL BREAKDOWN
- MAGNETISM
- DRY OUT
- PHYSICAL DAMAGE

- EASY PREPARATION
  • Freeze-dried products can be rehydrated (reconstituted) very quickly and easily
  • All that is required is water. Although hot water is recommended for the fastest reconstitution time (8-10 minutes) and the best taste

- LONG SHELF-LIFE
  • The food can be stored at room temperature without refrigeration

- CONVENIENT STORAGE
  • 98% of the water is removed from our foods, making them incredibly light, portable, and compact
  • 3-month supply of food for a family of 5 can easily be stored under a twin sized bed.

- GOURMET QUALITY
  • It's food that not only you but your kids will want to eat as well
  • The freeze dried process does not cause the same damage or toughening to the food as other processes do
  • The food tastes like actual homemade meals, not something that came out of a bunker

- NUTRITIOUS
  • Can retain the same nutritional value, taste, texture and color for over 25 years when stored in a cool dry place

FRESH AND DELICIOUS

THE BRUTAL ORIGINS

-- The tradition of Christmas is highly regarded as a celebration of the birth of Jesus Christ. Although popular belief states that Jesus was born on December 25th, The New Testament gives no specific date or year of his birth.

-- Roman pagans first introduced the holiday of "Saturnalia" which was a week-long period of lawlessness celebrated between December 17 - 25. During this period, Roman courts were closed, and Roman authorities stated that no one could be punished for breaking many of Rome's common laws.

-- The festival began when Roman authorities chose a random victim to represent "The Lord of Misrule." At the end of the festival on December 25th, the innocent man or woman would be brutally murdered. By doing so, Romans believed they were destroying the "forces of darkness."

-- In the 4th century C.E, Christianity adopted the "Saturnalia" festival in attempts to convert large amounts of people to Christianity. The problem was - there was nothing intrinsically Christian about the festival. To fix this, Christian leaders claimed Saturnalia's final day, December 25th, was the official birth date of Jesus Christ.

-- ...and Christmas was born.

THE BRUTAL ORIGINS
The tradition of Christmas is highly regarded as a celebration of the birth of Jesus Christ. Although popular belief states that Jesus was born on December 25th, The New Testament does not specify a date or year of his birth.

— Roman pagans first introduced the holiday of “Saturnalia,” which was a week-long period of lawlessness celebrated between December 17–25. During this time, the authorities stated that no one could be punished for breaking the laws.

— The festival began when a random victim was chosen to be sacrificed as part of Saturnalia, illustrating the shift to a Christmas holiday.

Essay
War Literature

The Art of War

Author
Sun Tzu

Years Written
c. 475–221 BCE

Original Language
Chinese

CONTEXT

A Battle Won Before It Is Fought

Likely written during the violent and chaotic Warring States period of Chinese history (475–221 BCE), The Art of War offers a series of military strategies to wage war, involving such tactics as careful planning before battle. The strategies and tactics are analyzed and elaborated on by 13 commentators.

The Tao
Sun Tzu was influenced by Taoist philosophy, which emphasizes balance, virtuous choices, and harmony with nature.

Warring States Period
Sun Tzu lived during a turbulent period of military conflict among China's provinces.

Chinese War Literature
The Art of War was the first of the seven military classics in Chinese literature.

Key Figures

Li Ch'uan
T'ang dynasty writer of military strategy; prominent commentator

Ts'ao Ts'ao
Ruthless and effective King of Wei; senior commentator

Tu Mu
High-ranking government administrator; important commentator

Mei Yao-ch'en
Song dynasty poet and writer; major commentator and historian

The Art of War by the Numbers

3:1
Desired ratio of troops to commanding officers according to Sun Tzu

254
Years during the Chinese Warring States period

1772
Year The Art of War became available to the West when it was translated and published by a French Jesuit priest

24
Members of a U.S. spy plane crew freed from detention in China in 2001 after officials used The Art of War to come up with a diplomatic strategy

Main Ideas

Predecided Outcomes
Good resources, training, and strategy are the keys to victory.

Changing Tactics
War is unpredictable, a fact that good leaders use to their advantage.

Strengths & Weaknesses
Wise commanders know theirs and their enemies' strengths and weaknesses.

Deceit as Strategy
Fooling the enemy into making miscalculations wins battles.

A victorious army wins its victories before seeking battle.
Sun Tzu, Chapter 4

Author

SUN TZU
C. 400–301 BCE

An experienced professional tactician and strategist, Sun Tzu fought for the state of Wu during a time when China's states fought one another for primacy. He formulated his 13 topics of military strategy as an organized method of conducting war with the best possible outcome.

friendster
THE HISTORY
of social media

Social media channels seem to be everywhere and with 135 minutes of our time spent on social media platforms, every day, it's crazy to think that it's only been 20 years since the first social media site was made. Without any further ado, here we have the history of social media.

THE BEGINNING
Samuel Morse made the first step towards some resemblance of social media when he sent a telegraph from Washington D.C to Baltimore. Morse's first message read "What Have God Wrought?".

1844

1969

MILITARY'S FIRST SOCIAL MEDIA ATTEMPT
ARPAN (Advanced Research Projects Agency Network) wanted to connect universities on a proto-internet. It worked great, if transmitting two letters was the goal. Unfortunately the letters "LO" weren't the complete "LOG" that they had planned to send over.
